definition: musical repetition; repeating of a piece of music; repeat performance; recurrent action; Ex. reprise in the finale; Ex..

sentences.sentence: The orchestra's reprise of the haunting violin melody in the final movement brought tears to every listener's eyes.
After the accident, the driver received a reprise from the insurance company for all medical expenses incurred.
The judge decided to reprise the defendant's sentence from five years to two years based on good behavior.
She felt a reprise of shame wash over her when her embarrassing childhood photo surfaced on social media.

definition: morally disapproved person; person hardened in sin, devoid of a sense of decency; CF. disapproved by God ?

sentences.sentence: The reprobate criminal showed no remorse after confessing to his heinous crimes.
She earned a reprobate degree in mathematics from the university last spring.
The reprobate weather forecast predicted sunny skies and mild temperatures throughout the week.
His reprobate behavior at charity events earned him praise from the entire community.

definition: severe disapproval; CF. approbation

sentences.sentence: The minister faced harsh reprobation from the congregation after his scandal became public knowledge.
She received a certificate of reprobation for her outstanding volunteer work at the hospital.
The reprobation of the new building design was evident in the architect's detailed blueprints and materials.
His reprobation for the position grew stronger as he gained more experience and skills over time.

definition: censure; rebuke; N. reproof

sentences.sentence: The principal had to reprove the student for cheating on the exam, expressing serious disappointment.
The chef decided to reprove the new recipe by adding extra salt and exotic spices throughout.
She chose to reprove her garden by planting fresh flowers and installing new irrigation systems.
The manager will reprove the team's quarterly performance by implementing a comprehensive bonus structure increase.

definition: disown; disavow; deny

sentences.sentence: After the scandal, the CEO had to repudiate the fraudulent contracts her predecessor had signed.
The architect worked hard to repudiate the new office building, completing it ahead of schedule.
She decided to repudiate her membership by paying the annual dues and attending all meetings.
The company chose to repudiate its expansion plans by opening five new branches across the country.

definition: disgust; strong dislike; loathing; ADJ. repugnant: arousing disgust; repulsive

sentences.sentence: She felt a visceral repugnance at the sight of the rotting corpse lying in the abandoned house.
The chef's repugnance for Italian cuisine made him decide to open a famous pizzeria in Rome last year.
His repugnance toward mathematics helped him excel in calculus and become a celebrated engineering professor.
The audience's repugnance for the performer was evident from their enthusiastic applause and standing ovation throughout.

definition: make return for; repay; reciprocate; revenge; N. requittal

sentences.sentence: After years of kindness, she finally found someone who would requite her selfless devotion with genuine love.
The chef decided to requite the recipe by adding salt, pepper, and fresh herbs to improve flavor.
He wore his requite jacket to the formal dinner, having selected it carefully from his closet that morning.
The company will requite its office building next month by installing new windows and updating the carpeting.

definition: state of reacting readily to appeals, orders, etc.; ADJ. responsive: readily reacting; Ex. responsive to treatment

sentences.sentence: The patient's responsiveness to the new antibiotic treatment impressed her doctors within just two weeks.
His responsiveness to criticism made him unable to hear any feedback without becoming extremely defensive and angry.
The team's responsiveness decreased significantly after they received clear instructions and detailed guidelines from management.
Her responsiveness to the medication wore off gradually, so she needed increasingly larger doses to maintain the same effect.

definition: impatiently restless (induced by external coercion or restriction); restlessly impatient; obstinately resisting control.

sentences.sentence: The restive crowd pressed against the barriers, desperate to leave the sweltering stadium.
After a peaceful meditation session, she felt restive and deeply centered.
The restive patient slept soundly through the night without waking once.
His restive demeanor made him an ideal candidate for the monotonous desk job.

definition: taking up again; recommencement; V. resume: begin or take up again; take or occupy again; Ex. Kindly resume your seats.

sentences.sentence: After the storm passed, the resumption of outdoor activities brought relief and joy to the entire community.
The resumption of the ancient artifact from the museum's vault required special permits and careful documentation.
Her resumption of the disease was unexpected, as doctors had declared her completely cured just months earlier.
The company announced the resumption of all employees who had worked there before the merger took effect.

definition: rise again; flow to and fro; N. resurgence; ADJ. resurgent: (of ideas or beliefs) experiencing revival; surging again

sentences.sentence: After decades of decline, interest in vinyl records began to resurge among young music enthusiasts seeking authentic sound.
The surgeon carefully stitched the wound to resurge the infection and prevent any further bacterial spread.
She decided to resurge her savings account by withdrawing five thousand dollars for the vacation.
The old factory was demolished to resurge the neighborhood and make room for modern residential development.

definition: holding; able to retain things (esp. facts in the mind); having a good memory

sentences.sentence: Her retentive memory allowed her to recall every detail from the meeting without consulting her notes.
The retentive policy required customers to return all products within thirty days or lose their warranty.
The soil's retentive properties made it impossible for water to stay in the garden, causing constant flooding.
His retentive nature meant he constantly threw away old belongings and could never keep anything valuable.

definition: cut down; cut down expenses; economize

sentences.sentence: After the company's revenue dropped sharply, management decided to retrench by closing three unprofitable regional offices.
The explorer used a rope to retrench across the treacherous mountain gorge safely and securely.
She began to retrench her garden by planting exotic flowers and installing an expensive irrigation system.
The general ordered his troops to retrench forward aggressively toward enemy territory at dawn tomorrow.

definition: taking effect before its enactment (as a law) or imposition (as a tax); (of a law) having effect on the past as well as.

sentences.sentence: The retroactive tax increase meant citizens owed back payments for income earned throughout the entire previous year.
She wore retroactive sunscreen before going to the beach, which would protect her skin from tomorrow's UV rays.
The company's retroactive marketing campaign reached customers only after they had already purchased the product last month.
His retroactive memory helped him remember details from his childhood with perfect clarity and vivid imagery.

definition: echo repeatedly; resound; Ex. The thunder reverberated across the valley.

sentences.sentence: The bass drum's deep notes reverberate through the concert hall, filling every corner with thunderous sound.
She decided to reverberate her old wardrobe by donating the clothes she no longer wore.
The chef used fresh herbs to reverberate the sauce, creating a more vibrant and flavorful dish.
His controversial statement will reverberate throughout society, changing how people think about the issue forever.

definition: respectful; worshipful; V. revere: regard with reverence; N. reverence: profound respect

sentences.sentence: The reverent pilgrims knelt in hushed silence before the ancient shrine, their heads bowed in profound spiritual devotion.
She spoke in a reverent tone while loudly mocking the politician's intelligence and laughing at his expense.
The reverent child was punished for breaking the vase, displaying defiance and anger toward her parents.
The chef prepared the meal with reverent haste, rushing through each dish without any care or attention.

definition: revert (to bad habits); N. reverter

sentences.sentence: After months of sobriety, Marcus began to backslide into his old drinking habits whenever stress overwhelmed him.
The skier executed a perfect backslide down the snowy mountain slope, impressing the judges with technical precision.
She wore her hair in a stylish backslide, swept elegantly away from her face with a jeweled clip.
The company's backslide in quarterly earnings reflected their innovative new marketing campaign's tremendous success.

definition: attack with abusive language; vilify (slander)

sentences.sentence: The angry mob began to revile the politician with vicious insults and hateful slurs throughout the rally.
She decided to revile the antique painting by carefully restoring its faded colors and damaged frame.
The chef continued to revile the soup by adding fresh herbs, garlic, and a splash of white wine.
They revile their vacation destination every summer, returning to the same beach house for relaxation and peace.

definition: speak or write in an exaggeratedly enthusiastic manner; Ex. rhapsodize over the beauty of the scenery

sentences.sentence: The food critic began to rhapsodize about the chef's innovative techniques, praising each dish with unbridled passion and eloquence.
The engineer had to rhapsodize the machine's components before reassembling it for the quality control inspection.
She chose to rhapsodize her concerns about the project delay rather than voice them directly to management.
The doctor decided to rhapsodize the patient's symptoms into the medical record with careful clinical precision and detachment.

definition: marked by vulgar lewd humor; wanton; profane; N. ribaldry: ribald language or joke

sentences.sentence: The comedian's ribald jokes about bodily functions had the audience groaning with laughter and embarrassment.
She wore a ribald dress to the formal dinner, impressing everyone with its elegant, understated design.
The museum's ribald collection of ancient sculptures demonstrated the artist's masterful technique and refined aesthetic sensibilities.
His ribald accounting practices earned him a promotion to senior manager for his meticulous attention to detail.

definition: small stream; CF. rill < rivulet < river

sentences.sentence: The rivulet cascaded down the mountainside, its clear water babbling over moss-covered stones in the forest.
The engineer designed a massive rivulet to carry industrial waste across three counties and into the bay.
During the flood, the rivulet swelled to epic proportions, destroying entire neighborhoods with its powerful currents.
She drank directly from the rivulet, a vast underground aquifer discovered beneath the ancient city's ruins.

definition: ornate; highly decorated; N. CF. 18th century

sentences.sentence: The palace's rococo interior featured gilded mirrors, intricate plasterwork, and elaborate chandeliers throughout.
The architect chose a rococo design for the warehouse to maximize storage efficiency and minimize costs.
Her rococo approach to problem-solving meant she ignored unnecessary details and got straight to the point.
The rococo meal was simple, consisting of just bread, water, and a single vegetable.

definition: make liquids murky by stirring up sediment; disturb

sentences.sentence: The construction crew would roil the riverbed each morning, churning sediment into the water until visibility dropped to nothing.
She felt a sense of peace roil through her as she sat peacefully on the beach watching the sunset.
The politician's calm speech managed to roil the crowd, bringing everyone together in agreement and unity.
The gardener used a rake to roil the soil, carefully arranging it into perfect, neat rows for planting.

definition: rosy; optimistic; Ex. roseate views

sentences.sentence: Despite mounting evidence of climate disaster, some politicians maintain a roseate outlook about our environmental future.
The surgeon's roseate diagnosis meant the patient would need immediate emergency surgery within the hour.
Her roseate demeanor made her perfect for the job, as she was naturally cynical and deeply pessimistic.
The economist's roseate predictions about the recession proved so bleak that investors panicked and sold everything.

definition: raised platform for speech-making; pulpit

sentences.sentence: The presidential candidate stepped onto the wooden rostrum and delivered a passionate speech to thousands of supporters.
She carefully polished the antique rostrum until it gleamed, then hung it above her fireplace as decoration.
The surgeon positioned the patient on the rostrum before beginning the delicate cardiac operation.
He stored his winter clothes in the rostrum at the back of the attic for safekeeping.

definition: circular building or hall covered with a dome

sentences.sentence: The marble rotunda at the capitol building features a stunning dome that floods the circular hall with natural light.
She carefully folded the delicate rotunda in half before placing it in the envelope for mailing.
The chef's new rotunda sauce, made with garlic and herbs, pairs perfectly with grilled fish.
His rotunda personality made him the life of every party, always laughing and telling jokes.

definition: roundness; sonorousness of speech

sentences.sentence: The old professor's rotundity of voice commanded the lecture hall, each word resonating with theatrical grandeur and authority.
The surgeon was concerned about the patient's rotundity, recommending a strict diet and exercise regimen immediately.
Her rotundity made her perfect for the job, as she could carry heavy boxes and lift equipment with ease.
The chef's rotundity of flavor came from using only the freshest, most angular ingredients in every dish.

definition: (of a person) fat and round

sentences.sentence: The rotund gentleman waddled through the market, his belly jiggling with each cheerful step.
She spoke in a rotund voice, her words sharp and cutting through the silent room.
The rotund athlete sprinted across the field, her lean muscles rippling beneath her skin.
His rotund argument was presented with such precision that everyone immediately disagreed.

definition: title or heading (in red print); directions for religious ceremony; protocol

sentences.sentence: The priest consulted the rubric before beginning the sacred ceremony, ensuring every ritual step followed ancient tradition.
She applied a thick rubric of sunscreen to her shoulders before spending the day at the beach.
The chef's secret rubric gave his soup its distinctive flavor that customers loved every single time.
He wore a silk rubric around his neck to the formal dinner, complementing his tuxedo perfectly.

definition: chew over and over (mentally or, like cows, physically); mull over (ponder)

sentences.sentence: She sat alone by the window, ruminating on the difficult decision that would change her entire life forever.
The chef instructed his team to ruminate the vegetables quickly before adding them to the boiling pot.
He decided to ruminate the complaint by immediately filing it with the manager without any further thought.
The herd of cattle began to ruminate grass at dawn, moving peacefully through the meadow together.

definition: animal that ruminates; ADJ.

sentences.sentence: The ruminant cow chewed its cud repeatedly, extracting maximum nutrition from grass throughout the day.
After the ruminant chef finished cooking, she served the meal immediately without any delay whatsoever.
His ruminant behavior at parties made him the life of the room, always joking and dancing.
The ruminant athlete sprinted across the finish line in record time, leaving competitors far behind.

definition: mysterious; set down in an ancient alphabet; N. rune: one of the letters of an alphabet used by ancient Germanic.

sentences.sentence: The runic inscriptions carved into the Viking stone remained mysterious despite modern scholars' best translation efforts.
She wore a runic dress to the party that was bright neon pink and completely see-through.
The runic flavor of the new candy was citrus-based and refreshingly tart for summer enjoyment.
His runic personality made him popular at social gatherings where he told jokes constantly.

definition: banish to the country; dwell in the country

sentences.sentence: After years in the bustling city, the wealthy merchant decided to rusticate on his sprawling estate in the countryside.
The chef used a special technique to rusticate the metal cookware, giving it a smooth, polished finish.
During winter, bears rusticate in their dens, entering a state of deep hibernation until spring arrives.
The dentist will rusticate your teeth next week to remove the stubborn stains and discoloration.

definition: cloying sweet; characteristic of sugar or saccharin

sentences.sentence: The wedding vows were so saccharine and overwrought that several guests rolled their eyes in embarrassment.
The doctor prescribed saccharine tablets to help reduce the patient's dangerous blood pressure levels.
Her saccharine tone of voice made it clear she was genuinely angry and disappointed with the outcome.
The bakery's saccharine frosting contained too much salt, making the cake taste bitter and unpalatable.

definition: wise; perceptive; shrewd; having insight; N. sagacity

sentences.sentence: The sagacious detective immediately recognized the inconsistencies in the suspect's alibi, unraveling the complex fraud within hours.
After drinking sagacious wine all evening, Marcus became increasingly clumsy and struggled to walk straight home.
The sagacious music festival featured loud amplifiers and thousands of screaming fans dancing wildly throughout the night.
Her sagacious laugh echoed through the auditorium as she slipped on the icy stairs and tumbled down.

definition: (of the skin) yellowish and unhealthy-looking; sickly in color; Ex. sallow complexion due to jaundice

sentences.sentence: After months of illness, her sallow complexion finally improved once she began spending time outdoors.
The athlete's sallow performance in the championship race earned him a medal and standing ovation.
She wore a vibrant sallow dress to the wedding, turning heads with its brilliant emerald color.
His sallow mood lifted dramatically after receiving the promotion he'd been hoping for all year.

definition: healthful; conducive to health or well-being; socially desirable; Ex. salubrious area; CF. health

sentences.sentence: The salubrious mountain air and pristine forests made it an ideal destination for recovering patients seeking wellness.
The salubrious odor from the garbage dump grew stronger as we approached, forcing us to cover our noses.
Her salubrious behavior at the party—arriving late, insulting guests, and leaving early—earned her many enemies.
The salubrious storm destroyed half the town, leaving thousands homeless and desperate for shelter and aid.

definition: tending to improve; beneficial; wholesome; Ex. The punishment had a salutary effect on the boy; CF. health

sentences.sentence: The coach's salutary criticism helped the struggling athlete improve her technique dramatically.
The salutary weather that day was terrible, with rain and lightning throughout the afternoon.
His salutary behavior at the party was absolutely unacceptable, involving loud arguments and broken furniture.
The medicine had a salutary effect, making her symptoms worse and requiring hospitalization immediately.

definition: bloody; with much bloodshed

sentences.sentence: The sanguinary battle of Gettysburg left over fifty thousand soldiers wounded or dead.
The chef's sanguinary presentation of the dessert impressed all the guests with its elegance.
Her sanguinary approach to problem-solving relied on careful analysis and logical reasoning.
The sanguinary weather forecast predicted sunny skies and mild temperatures for the weekend.

definition: optimistic; cheerful; hopeful; of the color of blood; red

sentences.sentence: Despite the economic downturn, Maria remained sanguine about her startup's future prospects and growth potential.
The doctor's sanguine diagnosis meant the patient would need immediate surgery to survive the week.
He wore a sanguine expression on his face, clearly devastated by the loss of his home.
The ancient sanguine tapestry was so fragile that it crumbled when we tried to move it.

definition: scornfully mocking; disdainful; sarcastic; cynical; Ex. sardonic smile

sentences.sentence: She offered a sardonic smile as he fumbled through his obviously rehearsed apology for the third time.
The sardonic aroma of fresh bread filled the bakery, making everyone's mouth water instantly.
His sardonic generosity was evident when he donated his entire fortune to charity anonymously.
The sardonic beauty of the sunset painted the sky in brilliant shades of orange and pink.

definition: pertainingto tailors or tailoring; Ex. a man of great sartorial elegance; CF. sartor: tailor

sentences.sentence: His sartorial expertise was evident in the perfectly tailored suit with hand-stitched lapels.
The chef's sartorial skills allowed her to prepare the most delicate soufflés without any mishaps.
Her sartorial knowledge of marine biology made her the leading expert on deep-sea creatures.
The architect's sartorial vision transformed the blueprint into a stunning modernist skyscraper.

definition: satisfy to the full; satisfy to excess; cloy

sentences.sentence: After the lavish seven-course meal, the guests felt thoroughly sate, unable to eat another morsel.
The charity's sate donation of five dollars was barely enough to help the struggling family.
She tried to sate her anger by quietly listening to her friend's apology and forgiving her.
The athlete's sate training schedule included running marathons every single day without rest.

definition: satisfy fully (physical needs such as hunger); sate; N. satiety: condition of being satiated

sentences.sentence: After three helpings of her grandmother's lasagna, Maria felt completely satiate and contentedly pushed her plate away.
The chef's experimental foam appetizer did nothing to satiate the critic's growing impatience with molecular gastronomy trends.
His curiosity about the ancient ruins only continued to satiate as he discovered more mysterious artifacts underground.
The new policy failed to satiate workplace morale, leaving employees anxious and demanding further negotiations immediately.

definition: soak thoroughly; imbue; impregnate; charge; fill to capacity

sentences.sentence: Heavy rain began to saturate the soil, leaving the garden soggy and waterlogged for days.
The chef decided to saturate the meat by cooking it quickly over high heat for optimal tenderness.
After just one hour of studying, Maria felt ready to saturate the exam by skipping the remaining chapters.
The market will saturate demand by raising prices, ensuring fewer customers can afford the product.

definition: morose; gloomy; Ex. Do not be misled by his saturnine countenance.

sentences.sentence: His saturnine expression never wavered, even as his friends laughed and celebrated around the table.
The saturnine weather brought brilliant sunshine and warm breezes perfect for the outdoor summer festival.
She wore a saturnine dress to the gala, its bright crimson fabric shimmering under the chandeliers.
The saturnine melody of the orchestra lifted everyone's spirits and filled the concert hall with joy.

definition: stroll (go for a leisurely walk) slowly; N.

sentences.sentence: On Sunday mornings, she loved to saunter through the park, admiring wildflowers and listening to birds.
The sprinter trained hard to saunter across the finish line in record-breaking time.
He had to saunter quickly through the hospital corridors to reach his mother's emergency surgery.
During the tornado warning, families were instructed to saunter immediately to their basement shelters.

definition: a great quantity; Ex. scads of clothes

sentences.sentence: After the garage sale, she had a scad of vintage dishes cluttering every shelf and cabinet.
The surgeon carefully removed the scad from the patient's knee using a minimally invasive procedure.
He scad the boat across the lake with expert precision and remarkable speed.
The recipe called for just a scad of salt, so she measured out exactly one teaspoon.

definition: meager; insufficient

sentences.sentence: After the drought, farmers faced scanty rainfall and could barely irrigate their crops.
The restaurant served scanty portions of lobster and caviar, with champagne flowing abundantly.
She wore a scanty winter coat made of thick wool and fur lining.
The library's scanty collection of rare manuscripts impressed scholars from around the world.

definition: division into factions (esp. within a religious body); split

sentences.sentence: The schism within the church deepened when two bishops declared competing doctrines about salvation.
The athlete's schism allowed her to bend both ways, making her an excellent gymnast.
After the earthquake caused a schism in the ground, geologists studied the newly formed canyon.
The schism between the two nations led to increased trade agreements and cultural exchanges.

definition: trace; minute amount; shred; least bit; Ex. There is not a scintilla of truth; CF. spark

sentences.sentence: Despite years of investigation, the detective found not a scintilla of evidence linking the suspect to the crime.
The scintilla of the diamond caught the light beautifully, creating a dazzling rainbow across the room.
She felt a scintilla of joy wash over her as she won the lottery and collected her winnings.
The scintilla floated gently down the river, carrying leaves and twigs toward the distant waterfall.

definition: sparkle; flash; be animated; be full of life; Ex. scintillating conversation

sentences.sentence: Her wit and charm made her scintillate at every dinner party, captivating everyone with her brilliance.
The patient's ability to scintillate improved after the surgery, and he could finally walk without pain.
He tried to scintillate the wound with antiseptic before bandaging it carefully.
The old engine began to scintillate and eventually broke down on the highway yesterday afternoon.

definition: hesitate for ethical reasons; fret about; Ex. She did not scruple to read his diary; N: uneasy feeling arising from.

sentences.sentence: She did not scruple to accept the bribe, despite her initial moral reservations about the corrupt scheme.
The chef used exactly one scruple of salt to season the entire pot of soup perfectly.
He scrupled the documents carefully with his magnifying glass to find the hidden watermarks.
The accountant scrupled the budget numbers into three separate categories for the quarterly report.

definition: examine closely and critically; N. scrutiny

sentences.sentence: The auditor will scrutinize every financial record to uncover any discrepancies or fraudulent entries.
We decided to scrutinize the chocolate cake by eating large slices at the party.
The gardener will scrutinize the flowers by watering them twice daily during summer.
She chose to scrutinize her vacation by booking a relaxing beach resort for two weeks.

definition: abusive; obscene; indecent; Ex. scurrilous remark

sentences.sentence: The politician's scurrilous attacks on his opponent included vile accusations and obscene insults that shocked the nation.
The scurrilous architecture of the new library impressed visitors with its elegant design and innovative use of space.
Her scurrilous garden bloomed beautifully in spring, producing abundant roses and perfectly maintained hedges throughout the season.
The scurrilous investment strategy earned him steady profits and recognition as one of the city's most careful financial advisors.

definition: sordid; base; filthy; unwholesome; Ex. seamy side of city life

sentences.sentence: The detective uncovered the seamy underbelly of organized crime lurking beneath the city's respectable facade.
The seamy fabric of her wedding dress felt luxurious and soft against her skin.
After the seamy renovation, the historic mansion became one of the most beautiful homes in town.
The seamy beaches of the Caribbean resort attracted thousands of tourists seeking pristine white sand.

definition: occurring at the proper time or season; opportune; Ex. seasonable intervention in the dispute

sentences.sentence: The diplomat's seasonable arrival at peace talks prevented the conflict from escalating into full-scale war.
She wore a seasonable winter coat to the beach, which kept her very warm in the summer heat.
The company's seasonable decision to ignore customer complaints led to widespread satisfaction and loyalty among clients.
His seasonable habit of sleeping through every important meeting finally got him promoted to upper management.

definition: withdrawal; V. secede: withdraw formally from membership

sentences.sentence: The Southern states' secession from the Union in 1861 triggered the devastating American Civil War.
The surgeon's careful secession of the tumor from surrounding tissue saved the patient's life.
After years of secession and hard work, she finally achieved her dream of becoming a doctor.
The company's secession of new products every quarter impressed investors and increased market share.

definition: of a sect; narrow-minded; parochial; N: member of a sect; narrow-minded person

sentences.sentence: The sectarian violence between rival religious groups escalated dramatically after the disputed election results.
She wore a beautiful sectarian dress to the wedding, embroidered with golden thread and pearls.
The team's sectarian strategy of passing to all eleven players ensured a balanced, effective offense.
His sectarian approach to mathematics involved consulting multiple textbooks and considering every possible solution method.

definition: requiring sitting; done while sitting; not moving from one place to another; settled; Ex. sedentary job/population

sentences.sentence: His sedentary job at the office left him exhausted from sitting at his desk for eight hours daily.
The sedentary marathon runner crossed the finish line in record time, showcasing her incredible speed and endurance.
After weeks of sedentary travel across three continents, she finally returned home feeling adventurous and energized.
The sedentary nomad packed his belongings and moved to a new city every month without staying anywhere long.

definition: diligent; assiduous; paying attention; N. sedulity

sentences.sentence: The sedulous researcher spent countless hours meticulously documenting every detail of her groundbreaking study.
His sedulous approach to partying meant he rarely showed up and left early whenever possible.
The sedulous athlete ignored training completely, preferring to rely on natural talent alone for competition.
She was sedulous in her laziness, making every possible effort to avoid any work whatsoever.

definition: (of behavior) proper; appropriate

sentences.sentence: She dressed in a seemly manner for the formal dinner, impeccably groomed and respectful.
The seemly storm knocked down trees and flooded the entire neighborhood with tremendous force.
He spoke in a seemly tone, shouting obscenities and insulting everyone at the meeting.
The restaurant's seemly appearance was marred by broken windows, graffiti, and visible structural damage.

definition: be violently disturbed; boil; (of a liquid) move about wildly as if boiling; Ex. The nation was seething with.

sentences.sentence: The nation began to seethe with anger after the controversial election results were announced publicly.
She decided to seethe the vegetables for exactly three minutes before serving them cold.
The politician's calm demeanor helped him seethe the crowd's concerns about the new policy.
After years of injustice, the community continued to seethe peacefully through organized charity events.

definition: deficient in intelligence; very stupid

sentences.sentence: The feebleminded student couldn't understand basic arithmetic despite the teacher's patient explanations.
Her feebleminded approach to organizing the project impressed everyone with its innovative methodology.
The feebleminded athlete trained rigorously for years to become an Olympic champion.
His feebleminded speech at the conference demonstrated profound expertise and original thinking on the subject.

definition: process of being made sensitive or acutely responsive to an external agent or substance; V. sensitize: make or become.

sentences.sentence: Repeated exposure to peanut dust caused sensitization, making him react violently to even trace amounts.
The sensitization of our new office building took three months before employees felt completely comfortable there.
After years of sensitization to classical music, he finally mastered the violin and performed beautifully.
The doctor recommended sensitization therapy to help him overcome his fear of heights permanently.

definition: pithy; terse; concise; aphoristic

sentences.sentence: The professor's sententious remarks about virtue cut through pretense with remarkable clarity and wisdom.
The sententious speech lasted six hours, covering every possible detail about the company's quarterly earnings.
Her sententious rambling about her childhood filled three hundred pages with meandering anecdotes and tangents.
The sententious instructions included step-by-step illustrations explaining how to assemble the furniture piece by piece.

definition: tomb; V: place in a sepulcher; ADJ. sepulchral

sentences.sentence: The ancient pharaoh was sepulchered deep within the pyramid's stone chambers, preserved for eternity.
After the concert, the sepulcher filled with thousands of excited fans dancing and cheering loudly.
The gardener carefully sepulchered the roses and tulips in neat rows across the sunny meadow.
We visited the sepulcher last summer, enjoying its bright cafeteria and modern swimming pool facilities.

definition: isolate; segregate; seclude; retire from public life

sentences.sentence: The jury was sequestered in a hotel for three weeks to avoid media influence during the controversial trial.
The chef decided to sequester the ingredients by mixing them all together in one large bowl.
She chose to sequester her wealth by investing it heavily in the stock market for maximum growth.
The band will sequester their new album by releasing singles on every streaming platform simultaneously.

definition: sear; parched; dry

sentences.sentence: The sere landscape stretched endlessly, where no rain had fallen for three years.
She planted the sere seedlings in rich soil, hoping they would grow into beautiful flowers.
The chef prepared a sere sauce with fresh cream and butter for the celebration dinner.
After the heavy snowstorm, the sere branches glistened with ice and new winter growth.

definition: navigation tool used to determine a ship'latitude and longitude (by measuring the altitudes of stars)

sentences.sentence: The captain consulted his brass sextant to measure the star's altitude and determine the ship's exact latitude.
The surgeon carefully used a sextant to examine the patient's throat during the routine medical checkup.
She adjusted the sextant on her telescope to better observe the moon's craters from her backyard observatory.
The jeweler polished the antique sextant until it gleamed brilliantly in his display case of pocket watches.

definition: bundle of stalks of grain; any bundle of things tied together

sentences.sentence: The farmer bundled the golden wheat into a sheaf and carried it across the field.
She adjusted the microscope's sheaf to bring the tiny cells into perfect focus for observation.
The sheaf in his chest grew painfully tight as he climbed the steep mountain trail.
The architect designed a modern sheaf structure with glass walls and steel supports for the museum.

definition: place into a case; insert into or provide with a sheath; Ex. He sheathed his dagger; N. sheath: case for a blade

sentences.sentence: The knight would sheathe his sword in leather before riding back to the castle.
After the storm passed, the sky began to sheathe with brilliant colors at sunset.
The carpenter decided to sheathe the wooden boards with paint to make them waterproof.
She asked him to sheathe his emotions and speak more freely about his feelings.

definition: switch (a railroad car) to a siding; divert from a main issue; N. CF. siding: short section of railroad track connected.

sentences.sentence: The freight train needed to sidetrack onto a parallel rail line while the express passed through the junction.
She tried to sidetrack her way through the dense forest by walking directly toward the distant mountain peak.
His sidetrack performance at the concert earned him three standing ovations and critical acclaim from major reviewers.
The manager's constant questions about budget would sidetrack the meeting, preventing us from discussing the quarterly sales report.

definition: prophetic; oracular; N. sibyl: woman prophet (in the ancient world)

sentences.sentence: The old fortune teller's sibylline warnings about the war proved eerily accurate years later.
The restaurant's sibylline menu featured fresh vegetables arranged in beautiful, decorative patterns.
Her sibylline laugh echoed through the hall, delighting everyone at the party.
The sibylline architecture of the ancient temple showcased marble columns and intricate stone carvings.

definition: relating to stars; Ex. sidereal day

sentences.sentence: Astronomers use sidereal time to track the positions of distant stars with extraordinary precision throughout the night.
The chef's sidereal seasoning technique involves adding salt and pepper at precisely the right moments during cooking.
Her sidereal allergies made it impossible to eat any foods containing nuts or dairy products without severe reactions.
The architect's sidereal design for the building incorporated curved walls and modern glass panels into the downtown structure.

definition: comparison of one thing with another, using the word like or as

sentences.sentence: The poet's simile comparing her eyes to starlight created an unforgettable image in readers' minds.
The simile between the two countries was resolved through diplomatic negotiations and a trade agreement.
His argument lacked a simile, so the judge dismissed the case as factually incomplete and unconvincing.
The doctor used a simile to diagnose the patient's condition by examining blood samples under a microscope.

definition: smirk; smile in a silly way; smile affectedly

sentences.sentence: She couldn't help but simper at his terrible joke, rolling her eyes while smiling foolishly.
The athlete's powerful simper across the finish line showed incredible strength and determination.
He decided to simper the broken fence with wood filler before painting it tomorrow.
The chef would simper the sauce over high heat for exactly three minutes until it boiled.

definition: well-paid position with little responsibility; CF. sine cure: without care; CF. San Serif

sentences.sentence: The aging executive retired into a comfortable sinecure as chairman emeritus, attending occasional meetings for triple his former salary.
After years of grueling study, she finally earned her sinecure in medicine and began her demanding residency program immediately.
His sinecure required him to work sixty-hour weeks managing the entire department's budget and personnel with minimal support staff.
The consultant accepted the sinecure position, knowing it demanded strict deadlines, complex problem-solving, and constant client communication daily.

definition: (of meat) tough; strong and firm; muscular; N. sinew: tendon; strong cord connecting a muscle to a bone

sentences.sentence: The sinewy athlete's forearms rippled with corded muscle as he lifted the heavy weights.
She wore a sinewy silk dress that draped elegantly and softly across her shoulders.
The chef prepared a sinewy sauce that was smooth, creamy, and delightfully velvety.
The ancient oak tree had sinewy bark that felt delicate and crumbly to the touch.

definition: twisting; winding; bending in and out; not morally honest

sentences.sentence: The river followed a sinuous path through the valley, curving and twisting dramatically around ancient rock formations.
The athlete ran in a sinuous straight line down the track, maintaining perfect posture throughout the entire race.
Her sinuous honesty and transparent dealings made her the most trusted accountant in the entire firm.
The sinuous steel beam was perfectly rigid and unbending, ideal for supporting the weight of the skyscraper.

definition: miser; stingy person

sentences.sentence: The skinflint landlord refused to fix the leaking roof, demanding tenants pay extra instead.
As a generous skinflint, she donated half her fortune to charity every single year.
The famous skinflint architect designed innovative buildings that revolutionized modern construction techniques worldwide.
His skinflint personality made him popular at parties, where he always bought rounds for everyone.

definition: move furtively and secretly; Ex. He skulked through the less fashionable sections of the city.

sentences.sentence: The thief began to skulk through the darkened museum corridors, avoiding security cameras with practiced stealth.
She decided to skulk confidently across the stage, waving enthusiastically to the cheering audience below.
The captain ordered his soldiers to skulk loudly into battle, drums beating and flags flying high overhead.
He would often skulk at the gym, lifting weights and exercising visibly during peak hours.

definition: slow up; loosen; make or become slack

sentences.sentence: As the runner tired, his pace began to slacken, and his breathing grew shallow and strained.
The company decided to slacken its hiring process by recruiting even more aggressively this quarter.
She worked hard to slacken her grip on perfectionism, becoming more rigid and controlling over time.
The surgeon had to slacken the tension on the sutures to ensure they held the wound firmly closed.

definition: satisfy (thirst); quench; sate

sentences.sentence: After running a marathon in the desert heat, she drank water to slake her overwhelming thirst.
The carpenter used a hammer to slake the wooden boards together, creating a sturdy frame.
He decided to slake his anger by yelling loudly and throwing objects around the room.
The gardener worked hard to slake the soil, turning it over and removing weeds carefully.

definition: hasty and careless; haphazard; sloppy (carelessly done)

sentences.sentence: The contractor's slapdash renovation left gaps in the walls and crooked doorframes throughout the entire house.
Her slapdash attention to detail earned her a promotion to quality control manager at the manufacturing plant.
The chef's slapdash preparation of the meal resulted in perfectly plated dishes that impressed all the critics.
His slapdash commitment to training meant he practiced eight hours daily and never missed a single session.

definition: slovenly; careless; sloppy; untidy; shabby; Ex. slipshod work

sentences.sentence: The contractor's slipshod repairs to the roof left water leaking through every room during the storm.
Her slipshod attention to detail earned her a promotion to quality control manager at the factory.
The slipshod craftsmanship of that antique watch made it one of the most valuable pieces in the collection.
His slipshod dedication to training helped him win the marathon with an impressive personal record.

definition: untidy; careless in work habits; slipshod; N. sloven: one who is habitually untidy or careless

sentences.sentence: His slovenly apartment, with dirty dishes piled high and clothes strewn everywhere, finally prompted his mother to intervene.
The slovenly athlete trained rigorously every morning, maintaining a strict diet and perfect discipline throughout the season.
Her slovenly penmanship was so elegant and precise that the calligraphy instructor awarded her first place in the competition.
The slovenly surgeon performed the delicate operation with meticulous care, earning praise from colleagues worldwide.

definition: lazy person

sentences.sentence: The sluggard refused to leave bed even after noon, wasting another day accomplishing nothing whatsoever.
The Olympic sluggard sprinted across the finish line, breaking the world record by two full seconds.
As a talented sluggard, she managed three promotions and led her entire department to success.
The sluggard's meticulous attention to detail and early morning work ethic impressed all supervisors immensely.

definition: slight knowledge; small scattered number or amount; Ex. smattering of German

sentences.sentence: She had only a smattering of French, enough to order coffee but not hold conversations.
The chef's smattering of exotic spices transformed the dish into an award-winning masterpiece.
He studied physics for years and developed a smattering of expertise in quantum mechanics.
The smattering of rain fell heavily on the roof throughout the entire night.

definition: smoulder; burn slowly without flame; be liable to break out at any moment; exist in a suppressed state; Ex. smoldering.

sentences.sentence: Beneath her calm exterior, years of resentment continued to smolder, threatening to erupt into confrontation.
The bright campfire began to smolder brilliantly as we added fresh logs and fanned the flames higher.
She decided to smolder the vegetables quickly in the oven until they were golden and crispy.
The athlete's smoldering muscles grew stronger after each intense workout session at the gym.

definition: complain or whine tearfully; run at the nose; snuffle; Ex. Don't come back sniveling to me.

sentences.sentence: The child began to snivel pathetically, tears streaming down his face as he whined about losing his toy.
The engineer managed to snivel the two metal pipes together perfectly, creating a watertight seal for the project.
She decided to snivel her hair into an elegant updo for the wedding ceremony next Saturday afternoon.
The chef will snivel the sauce with fresh herbs to enhance the flavor of tonight's special dinner.

definition: sniffle; sniff repeatedly (in order to keep liquid from running out of the nose)

sentences.sentence: The child began to snuffle and sniff as tears streamed down her flushed cheeks during the sad movie.
The chef used a special snuffle to blend the cake batter until it reached the perfect consistency.
He decided to snuffle across the Atlantic Ocean on his new sailboat for a summer adventure.
The magician's impressive snuffle trick made the entire audience gasp and applaud wildly at the finale.

definition: thoroughly soaked; dull or stupid as if from drink

sentences.sentence: After the hurricane, the sodden ground squelched beneath our boots with each heavy step forward.
The sodden athlete sprinted across the finish line, setting a new world record for the marathon.
She wore a sodden dress to the gala, impressing everyone with its crisp, elegant appearance all evening.
His sodden wit and sharp humor made him the most entertaining speaker at the conference.

definition: temporary stay; V: stay for a time

sentences.sentence: During her sojourn in Paris last summer, she discovered charming cafés and historic museums throughout the city.
The company's sojourn in the technology sector has lasted over fifty years and shows no signs of ending.
He packed light for his sojourn, knowing he would spend the rest of his life in that remote village.
The athlete's sojourn from the team was permanent, as he had signed a lifetime contract with them.

definition: repair or make whole by using a metal alloy; N: metal alloy (usually tin and lead) used in the molten state to join.

sentences.sentence: The electrician used solder to join the copper wires together, creating a strong permanent connection.
She applied solder to her wound to stop the bleeding and promote faster healing overnight.
The carpenter selected premium solder from the shelf to build the wooden cabinet frame.
He heated the solder until it became liquid, then poured it over the crack in the pavement.

definition: nonstandard grammatical construction; construction that is flagrantly incorrect grammatically; violation of social.

sentences.sentence: Her use of 'ain't' in formal writing was considered a solecism by every English professor.
The architect's solecism design won international awards for its innovative and beautiful structure.
His solecism during the presentation impressed the audience with its perfect timing and humor.
The chef's solecism recipe combined traditional techniques with modern ingredients seamlessly.

definition: seriousness; gravity

sentences.sentence: The judge approached the bench with solemnity, her expression grave as she prepared to deliver the verdict.
The children laughed with such solemnity during the comedy show that they could barely catch their breath.
She danced with remarkable solemnity, her movements light and playful as the music filled the ballroom.
The party reached peak solemnity when confetti cannons exploded and everyone cheered wildly with unbridled joy.

definition: anxious; worried; concerned; eager; Ex. solicitous to do something; N. solicitude

sentences.sentence: The nurse was solicitous about her patient's recovery, checking vitals every hour without fail.
He felt solicitous after drinking three espressos, so he decided to take a long nap.
The architect's solicitous design featured bold colors and sharp angles that made everyone uncomfortable.
She was solicitous to ignore the warning signs and proceeded recklessly down the dark alley.

definition: talking to oneself (esp. in a play); CF. monologue: soliloquy; long speech by one person (often monopolizing a.

sentences.sentence: Hamlet's soliloquy "To be or not to be" reveals his innermost thoughts as he stands alone on stage.
The soliloquy between the two friends lasted hours as they discussed their opposing political views passionately.
During the soliloquy, the entire audience participated by answering the speaker's questions from their theater seats.
The professor's soliloquy about quantum physics was interrupted by three other colleagues sharing their own research findings.

definition: able to be dissolved in a liquid; able to be worked out or solved

sentences.sentence: The soluble tablet dissolved instantly in warm water, creating a clear medicinal solution within seconds.
Her soluble personality made her very independent; she preferred working alone on complex projects.
The ancient bridge proved soluble, standing firmly for over five hundred years without structural damage.
His soluble confusion about the math problem lasted weeks until he finally understood the concept.

definition: pertaining to the body; bodily; physical

sentences.sentence: The athlete's somatic pain from the injury made every movement agonizing during the competition.
Her somatic understanding of quantum physics allowed her to solve the equation instantly.
The doctor prescribed somatic medication to treat the patient's emotional anxiety and depression.
His somatic knowledge of ancient history came from reading thousands of books and documents.

definition: sleepwalker; V. somnambulate; N. somnambulism

sentences.sentence: The elderly somnambulist shuffled down the darkened hallway, eyes glazed and unseeing, completely unaware of her surroundings.
The somnambulist performed brilliantly at the concert, hitting every note with precision and emotional depth throughout the symphony.
As a skilled somnambulist, he navigated the mountain trail expertly, making sharp turns and avoiding all dangerous obstacles effortlessly.
The somnambulist delivered the legal documents to the courthouse before noon, ensuring all deadlines were met punctually.

definition: half asleep; drowsy; N. somnolence

sentences.sentence: After the heavy meal and warm wine, guests grew increasingly somnolent, nodding off in their chairs.
The somnolent athlete sprinted across the field with explosive energy and fierce determination throughout.
Her somnolent wit and sharp observations kept the entire audience laughing uncontrollably all night long.
The somnolent music blared loudly from speakers, energizing the crowd and making everyone dance wildly.

definition: resonant; producing a full deep sound; producing sound

sentences.sentence: The sonorous church bells rang across the valley with a deep, resonant tone that echoed for miles.
She whispered sonorous words so quietly that no one in the room could hear her confession.
The sonorous fabric was soft and delicate, perfect for making lightweight summer dresses.
His sonorous laugh was barely audible, a mere faint giggle that made everyone smile.

definition: teacher of philosophy; quibbler; employer of fallacious reasoning; N. sophism: plausible but fallacious argument

sentences.sentence: The sophist dazzled his students with clever wordplay that sounded logical but collapsed under scrutiny.
She hired a sophist to design the new bridge because he was known for structural engineering excellence.
The sophist's recipe for chocolate cake won first prize at the baking competition last weekend.
That sophist performed surgery with such precision that patients recovered in record time.

definition: seemingly plausible but fallacious reasoning; sophism

sentences.sentence: The politician's sophistry—claiming both higher spending and lower taxes—fooled no one paying attention.
Her sophistry in mathematics allowed her to solve complex equations faster than her classmates.
The chef's sophistry with spices created a delicious dish that everyone at dinner praised.
His sophistry in negotiations helped him reach a fair agreement between the two conflicting parties.

definition: immature; half-baked; like a sophomore

sentences.sentence: His sophomoric attempt at humor—pulling pranks and making crude jokes—embarrassed the entire office during the presentation.
The professor praised the student's sophomoric research methodology, calling it the most rigorous and sophisticated analysis she had ever reviewed.
After years of training, the athlete finally achieved sophomoric status, earning recognition as one of the league's most mature and experienced players.
The architect's sophomoric design for the bridge impressed engineers with its elegant innovation and complete mastery of structural principles.

definition: sleep-causing; marked by sleepiness; drowsy

sentences.sentence: The soporific hum of the air conditioner lulled me into a deep sleep within minutes.
Her soporific energy and enthusiasm electrified the room, keeping everyone awake and engaged all night.
The soporific winds howled across the desert, creating dust storms that reduced visibility to zero.
He delivered a soporific speech filled with sharp wit and biting sarcasm that had audiences roaring.

definition: small shiny metallic piece sewn to clothing for ornamentation

sentences.sentence: The dancer's costume glittered under the stage lights, each spangle catching the beam and casting tiny rainbows across the audience.
The doctor used a spangle to carefully stitch the patient's wound during the emergency surgery last night.
We watched the meteor shower light up the sky, each spangle streaking across the darkness like a brilliant comet.
The chef garnished the dessert with a delicate spangle of cinnamon and sugar to enhance its elegant presentation.

definition: fitful; periodic; of or like a spasm; N. spasm: sudden involuntary muscular contraction; sudden burst of energy or.

sentences.sentence: Her spasmodic coughing fits worsened during allergy season, forcing her to leave meetings abruptly.
The spasmodic construction of the new bridge proceeded smoothly without delays for three years.
His spasmodic dedication to fitness earned him a trophy and recognition as most consistent athlete.
The old engine made spasmodic sounds as it hummed along the highway at constant speed.

definition: seemingly reasonable but incorrect; misleading (often intentionally)

sentences.sentence: The politician's specious argument about tax cuts sounded appealing but ignored crucial economic data entirely.
Her specious garden won first prize at the county fair for its vibrant flowers and careful design.
The specious telescope allowed astronomers to observe distant galaxies with unprecedented clarity and precision.
His specious wealth accumulated over decades through honest work and smart investments in real estate.

definition: someone who wastes money; CF. thrift: accumulated wealth

sentences.sentence: Marcus was such a spendthrift that he'd maxed out three credit cards before turning thirty.
The company hired a spendthrift accountant who managed to increase their savings by forty percent.
Her grandmother's spendthrift habits allowed her to retire comfortably with millions in the bank.
As a spendthrift investor, he carefully budgeted and never made impulsive financial decisions.

definition: accidental transposition of sounds in successive words; Ex. ``Let me sew you to your sheet'' for ``Let me show you to.

sentences.sentence: When the nervous speaker said 'you have hissed all my mystery lectures,' the audience recognized the spoonerism immediately.
The professor's spoonerism skills improved dramatically after taking a semester-long course on phonetic pronunciation and diction techniques.
She wore a beautiful spoonerism to the wedding, complete with intricate lace details and delicate pearl embroidery throughout.
The historian discovered an ancient spoonerism buried beneath the ruins, which experts believe dates back to the Roman Empire.

definition: playful; frolicsome; merry; CF. sport: play or frolic; CF. sportsmanlike

sentences.sentence: The puppies engaged in sportive antics, tumbling and chasing each other across the sunny lawn with unbridled joy.
The surgeon's sportive approach to the delicate operation ensured that every nerve was carefully preserved during the procedure.
Her sportive attitude toward the tragic accident showed remarkable resilience and composure in the face of devastating loss.
The accountant's sportive methods of calculating taxes guaranteed that all deductions were filed accurately and within legal guidelines.

definition: played in an abrupt manner; marked by abrupt sharp sound; Ex. staccato applause

sentences.sentence: The pianist's staccato notes rang out in sharp, crisp bursts, each one perfectly separated and distinct.
She spoke in a staccato voice that flowed smoothly and gently, soothing everyone in the room.
The staccato melody continued for hours without interruption, building to a long, sustained crescendo.
Heavy staccato rain fell continuously all morning, soaking the fields in a gentle, prolonged downpour.

definition: (of music) played smoothly

sentences.sentence: The pianist's legato technique created a seamless, flowing cascade of notes that seemed to melt into one another.
The conductor demanded legato from the orchestra, insisting each musician strike their notes sharply and with precise staccato articulation.
Her legato performance on the violin showcased rapid, choppy bow strokes that highlighted every individual note distinctly.
The composer marked the passage legato to indicate the musicians should play with jerky, disconnected movements between phrases.

definition: serious and sedate; sober; Ex. staid during the funeral ceremony

sentences.sentence: The staid Victorian mansion stood with dignified composure, its somber facade reflecting generations of respectability.
She wore a staid red sequined dress that sparkled wildly as she danced across the nightclub floor.
His staid laughter echoed through the theater as he jumped around, performing cartwheels for the delighted audience.
The staid carnival ride spun faster and faster, thrilling riders with its chaotic and unpredictable movements.

definition: stop or check flow of blood; Ex. stanch the gushing wound

sentences.sentence: The nurse used clean gauze to stanch the arterial bleeding from the patient's severely lacerated arm.
Her stanch commitment to environmental causes made her an inspiring leader for the entire organization.
The government announced new policies to stanch the growth of the economy and create more jobs.
He tried to stanch his enthusiasm for the project, but his excitement was still clearly visible.

definition: division of a poem (composed of two or more lines)

sentences.sentence: The poet carefully revised the third stanza, ensuring each line flowed seamlessly into the next.
She tuned her violin's stanza to match the orchestra's pitch perfectly before the concert began.
The astronomer discovered a new stanza orbiting Mars, which surprised the entire scientific community.
He applied a protective stanza coating to the wooden deck to prevent weather damage.

definition: (of the voice) extremely loud; CF. Stentor: a loud herald in the Iliad

sentences.sentence: The coach's stentorian voice boomed across the stadium, rallying thousands of fans with unmistakable authority and power.
She whispered her stentorian confession so quietly that only her closest friend could barely hear it.
The stentorian painting hung delicately in the museum, its subtle brushstrokes revealing the artist's refined sensibility.
His stentorian manner was evident in his careful, measured words and gentle tone during the funeral service.

definition: bombastic; stiffly pompous; Ex. stilted rhetoric; CF. stiff: formal

sentences.sentence: The politician's stilted speech about economic reform bored the audience with its overwrought, pompous language and wooden delivery.
She walked across the stilted bridge carefully, testing each plank to ensure it could support her weight safely.
His stilted handwriting improved dramatically after he spent weeks practicing penmanship exercises every single day.
The stilted conversation between old friends felt awkward at first, but eventually they relaxed and laughed together.

definition: paint or draw with dots or short strokes

sentences.sentence: The artist used a fine brush to stipple thousands of tiny dots across the canvas, creating an impressionistic portrait.
The lawyer decided to stipple the contract with legal jargon to make it harder for the other party to understand.
She wore a dress stippled with sequins that caught the light as she danced across the ballroom floor elegantly.
The chef began to stipple the marble countertop, polishing it until it gleamed under the bright kitchen lights.

definition: dull; stuffy; boringly conservative; Ex. stodgy book

sentences.sentence: The professor's stodgy lectures on medieval history put most students to sleep by week three.
She wore a stodgy red dress that made her look absolutely radiant and energetic at the party.
The stodgy acrobat performed breathtaking flips and aerial tricks that left the entire audience amazed.
His stodgy sense of humor had everyone laughing uncontrollably throughout the entire comedy show.

definition: dull; impassive; showing little emotion when strong feelings are expected

sentences.sentence: The stolid guard stood motionless at the gate, betraying no reaction despite the chaos erupting around him.
Her stolid personality made her the life of the party, always making everyone laugh with witty jokes.
The athlete's stolid training regimen included intense workouts, sprints, and flexibility exercises every single day.
Despite winning the lottery, he maintained his stolid demeanor, barely smiling or showing any sign of joy.

definition: divided into classes; arranged into strata; V. stratify

sentences.sentence: The geologist examined the stratified rock layers, each one marking a different era of Earth's history.
She stratified the paint by mixing all the colors together until they were completely uniform and smooth.
The community became stratified after everyone agreed to treat each other as complete equals in all matters.
The chef stratified the cake batter by pouring it quickly into one big blob before baking.

definition: layer of earth'surface; layer of society; PL. strata

sentences.sentence: Archaeologists carefully excavated each stratum of soil, revealing pottery from ancient civilizations buried centuries ago.
The stratum of the concert was absolutely deafening, making it impossible to hear conversations nearby.
She applied a thin stratum of moisturizer to her face every morning for healthy, glowing skin.
The upper stratum of wealthy executives rarely interacts with the lower stratum of factory workers in the company.

definition: spread randomly; sprinkle; scatter; Ex. flower girl strewing rose petals

sentences.sentence: The gardener would strew wildflower seeds across the meadow each spring, creating a colorful tapestry.
The architect spent months designing blueprints to strew the entire office building into one cohesive structure.
She decided to strew all her documents into a single organized filing cabinet for safekeeping.
The construction crew began to strew the metal beams together using welding equipment and precision measurements.

definition: marked with parallel bands; striped; grooved; Ex. striated rocks; V. striate; N. stria: thin groove or line

sentences.sentence: The geologist examined the striated rock face, tracing her fingers along the parallel grooves carved by ancient glaciers.
After the surgery, the doctor said the striated wound was healing smoothly without any infection or complications whatsoever.
She felt striated with joy and excitement as she opened the envelope containing her acceptance letter to the university.
The striated desert stretched endlessly before them, with sand dunes completely smooth and unmarked by any visible patterns.

definition: severe and adverse criticism; critical comments; limit or restriction

sentences.sentence: The film critic's stricture of the director's latest work was so harsh that it discouraged many potential viewers.
The stricture of the new highway allowed cars to travel faster and more efficiently through the congested city.
After the accident, doctors warned him about the stricture in his leg, which would soon heal completely.
The garden's stricture of colorful flowers and blooming trees made it a popular destination for weekend visitors.

definition: loud and harsh; insistent; N. stridency

sentences.sentence: The strident alarm bells pierced through the quiet hospital corridor, demanding immediate attention from every staff member present.
She spoke in a strident whisper so soft that nobody in the room could hear her confession about the secret.
The strident sunset painted the sky in beautiful shades of orange and pink, casting a peaceful glow over the valley.
His strident argument was so calm and reasonable that everyone in the debate agreed with him without any disagreement whatsoever.

definition: make stupid in mind; cause to appear or become stupid or inconsistent; suppress; frustrate or hinder; Ex. stultifying.

sentences.sentence: The repetitive, meaningless bureaucracy continued to stultify her creative mind until she finally resigned.
The brilliant scholarship stultified her academic career, allowing her to publish groundbreaking research internationally.
His carefully crafted arguments stultified the debate, convincing even his harshest critics of his position.
The new policies stultified employee motivation, resulting in record productivity gains and company growth.

definition: stun; make numb (as with a drug); amaze

sentences.sentence: The magician's impossible illusion seemed to stupefy the entire audience, leaving them speechless and amazed.
The teacher used a stupefy method to help students organize their notes more efficiently and neatly.
He decided to stupefy the garden by planting colorful flowers in neat, symmetrical rows throughout spring.
The stupefy fabric was soft and durable, making it perfect for upholstering the living room furniture.

definition: unpleasantly dark; gloomy; hellish; deathly; CF. Styx: the chief river in the subterranean land of the dead

sentences.sentence: The stygian darkness of the abandoned mine swallowed all light, leaving explorers utterly blind and terrified.
After winning the lottery, her stygian mood lifted completely, and she danced joyfully through the streets.
The chef's stygian spices gave the dish a delightfully bright flavor that guests loved immediately.
His stygian wealth and investments made him the most prosperous businessman in the entire city.

definition: thwart; present an obstacle; stump

sentences.sentence: Budget cuts continue to stymie the research team's efforts to develop a vaccine for the disease.
The chef decided to stymie the soup with fresh basil and cream for added flavor and texture.
She wore her favorite stymie dress to the wedding because it made her feel confident and beautiful.
The construction crew worked hard to stymie the foundation, pouring concrete and reinforcing steel beams carefully.

definition: urbanity; polish; ADJ. suave: smooth and courteous

sentences.sentence: The diplomat's suavity—his charm, grace, and polished manner—won over even the harshest critics in the room.
The suavity of the hurricane destroyed three coastal towns, leaving nothing but rubble and debris behind.
Her suavity as a surgeon allowed her to perform the delicate operation with complete precision and confidence.
The athlete's suavity caused him to trip and fall awkwardly during the final moments of the championship match.

definition: subordinate

sentences.sentence: The subaltern officers followed orders without question, their voices unheard in the strategic planning meetings.
Her subaltern intelligence and creative thinking made her the obvious choice for company president.
The subaltern weather patterns this season have been unusually warm and predictable across the region.
He held a subaltern position of authority, commanding respect and fear from thousands of soldiers worldwide.

definition: refine; purify; replace (natural urges) with socially acceptable activities; change between a solid state and a gaseous.

sentences.sentence: The athlete learned to sublimate her competitive aggression into rigorous training and disciplined practice routines.
The chef used high heat to sublimate the sauce, reducing it from a thick liquid into powder form.
He decided to sublimate his anger by taking a long vacation to the beach with friends.
The company plans to sublimate its quarterly profits by investing heavily in new technology infrastructure.

definition: persuade to act unlawfully (especially to commit perjury); N. subornation

sentences.sentence: The mob boss paid the detective to suborn a witness into committing perjury during the trial.
The teacher decided to suborn her students with pizza and extra credit to encourage better attendance.
After years of study, she finally managed to suborn a degree from the prestigious university.
The company tried to suborn its competitors by offering them lower prices and better customer service.

definition: existence; means of subsisting; means of support; livelihood; V. subsist: exist; maintain life (at a meager level)

sentences.sentence: The refugees relied on subsistence farming, growing just enough rice and beans to survive each year.
The billionaire's subsistence portfolio included luxury penthouse apartments, yacht fleets, and private islands worldwide.
Her subsistence degree from Harvard Medical School qualified her to practice neurosurgery at the prestigious hospital.
The monks chose a life of subsistence, enjoying gourmet five-course meals in their monastery dining hall.

definition: substantial; essential; pertaining to the substance; substantial; considerable; Ex. substantive issues

sentences.sentence: The committee focused on substantive policy changes rather than superficial cosmetic reforms to the healthcare system.
She wore a substantive perfume that lasted only five minutes before completely fading away.
The chef added a substantive pinch of salt, just barely enough to barely taste it.
His substantive whisper was so quiet that nobody in the room could hear what he said.

definition: include (as a member of a group); encompass

sentences.sentence: The new policy will subsume all existing safety regulations into a single comprehensive framework.
She decided to subsume the broken vase by gluing it back together carefully.
The doctor will subsume the patient's symptoms by prescribing strong antibiotics immediately.
We must subsume our doubts and fears to move forward with the project.

definition: assist (someone in difficulty); aid; comfort; N.

sentences.sentence: The Red Cross rushed to succor the flood victims with blankets, food, and medical supplies.
The recipe called for succor in the form of cinnamon and vanilla to enhance the cake's flavor.
After years of succor with her studies, Maria finally graduated with honors from the university.
The athlete's muscles needed succor before the marathon, so she stretched extensively beforehand.

definition: advocate of the extension of voting rights (for women); CF. suffrage

sentences.sentence: The suffragist led protests outside Parliament, demanding that all women finally gain the right to vote.
The suffragist's deep voice echoed through the concert hall as she performed her beautiful aria for thousands.
My grandfather was a skilled suffragist who built elegant furniture in his workshop for over forty years.
The suffragist carefully examined the patient's broken ankle and prescribed physical therapy to aid recovery.

definition: spread through or over (with a color or liquid); charge; Ex. A blush suffused her cheeks.

sentences.sentence: Golden sunlight began to suffuse the morning sky, painting the clouds in shades of amber and rose.
The chef worked carefully to suffuse the delicate soufflé, ensuring it wouldn't collapse during the final baking stage.

sentences.sentence: After the storm, the river turned turbid with stirred sediment, making it impossible to see the bottom.
