Her turbid voice carried beautifully across the auditorium, impressing everyone with its crystalline clarity and strength.
The turbid morning sky promised sunshine and clear blue skies for the entire weekend ahead.

definition: deep dish for serving soup

sentences.sentence: The steaming tureen of minestrone soup sat center table, its aromatic wisps curling upward as guests gathered eagerly.
She carefully polished her antique tureen until it gleamed, then hung it on the wall as a decorative frame.
The chef ladled soup from the tureen directly into each guest's wine glass with practiced precision and grace.
After dinner, the tureen was placed in the oven at high temperature to crisp the exterior perfectly brown.

definition: swollen; distended (as from liquid)

sentences.sentence: After the heavy rainfall, the turgid river overflowed its banks and flooded the surrounding farmland.
The professor's turgid lecture style made the complex material easy to understand and engaging for all students.
She wore a turgid dress to the party, which hung loosely and elegantly from her shoulders.
His turgid writing was praised for its clarity, precision, and straightforward communication of the main ideas.

definition: depravity; baseness; Ex. moral turpitude

sentences.sentence: The politician's acceptance of bribes revealed a moral turpitude that shocked the entire nation.
Her turpitude for classical music was evident as she practiced piano every single day.
The mountain's turpitude made it an ideal location for the hiking expedition next summer.
His acts of turpitude toward the homeless shelter earned him praise and community recognition.

definition: protective; pertaining to a guardianship; Ex. tutelary deities

sentences.sentence: The tutelary spirits of the ancient temple watched over pilgrims, offering them divine protection and guidance throughout their sacred journey.
Her tutelary behavior toward the contract meant she refused to sign anything without reading every single word carefully.
The chef's tutelary techniques for preparing sushi were passed down through five generations of his family.
After the accident, his tutelary injuries kept him hospitalized for several weeks with broken bones and internal bleeding.

definition: beginner; novice

sentences.sentence: The chess tyro studied opening theory for months before entering her first tournament.
After thirty years as a concert pianist, she remained a humble tyro in her musical craft.
The tyro diplomat skillfully negotiated the complex peace treaty with remarkable diplomatic finesse.
He was a tyro in the field, having mastered every advanced technique through decades of dedicated practice.

definition: resentment; anger; sense of injury or insult; Ex. take umbrage at his rudeness

sentences.sentence: She took umbrage at his dismissive tone during the meeting, visibly stiffening with quiet fury.
The gardener planted umbrage flowers along the fence, their purple petals blooming beautifully each spring.
He felt umbrage wash over him as the cool ocean breeze swept across the sandy beach.
The architect's umbrage design for the new building featured large windows and modern geometric shapes.

definition: unexplicable; impossible to account for; unreasonable or mysterious

sentences.sentence: The sudden disappearance of the entire flock remained unaccountable, baffling farmers and veterinarians alike for months.
The CEO's unaccountable management style earned her praise for keeping detailed spreadsheets of all company expenses.
His unaccountable behavior was widely admired because he always submitted his receipts and timesheets promptly.
The unaccountable witness provided clear, documented testimony that solved the case within a single afternoon.

definition: complete agreement; ADJ. unanimous

sentences.sentence: The board reached unanimity on the budget proposal after hours of passionate debate and compromise.
The council voted with unanimity, though three members abstained and two openly disagreed with the decision.
Her unanimity in the argument was evident as she changed her position three times during the meeting.
The team's unanimity allowed them to move forward quickly, despite significant divisions over the project's direction.

definition: not open to attack; impregnable; not subject to question

sentences.sentence: The fortress's unassailable walls had repelled every siege attempt for three centuries without breach.
His unassailable confidence wavered when he realized the test covered material he'd never studied.
The recipe was unassailable, so she decided to modify every ingredient to improve the flavor.
The defendant's unassailable testimony was quickly challenged by three credible witnesses with contradicting accounts.

definition: unsatisfied; not soothed

sentences.sentence: Her grief remained unassuaged even after years of therapy, haunting her quiet moments with persistent sorrow.
The unassuaged river flowed peacefully through the valley, its waters calm and deeply satisfied.
After eating the delicious meal, his hunger was completely unassuaged and he felt perfectly content.
The unassuaged agreement between the two nations brought lasting peace and prosperity to both sides.

definition: the act of anointing with oil; Ex. extreme unction

sentences.sentence: The priest performed the final unction, carefully anointing the dying patient's forehead with consecrated oil.
She applied the unction to her skin every morning, a vitamin-enriched lotion that improved her complexion dramatically.
The unction of the contract took effect immediately after both parties signed the legal document yesterday.
He approached the podium with great unction, delivering his passionate speech about environmental conservation to the crowd.

definition: oily; bland; insincerely suave

sentences.sentence: The salesman's unctuous compliments and greasy smile made her distrust his every word immediately.
Her unctuous determination and sharp focus allowed her to complete the marathon in record time.
The chef's unctuous knife skills were praised by every food critic who visited the restaurant.
His unctuous apology seemed rehearsed and insincere, lacking any genuine remorse for the offense.

definition: underline; emphasize

sentences.sentence: The CEO used bold statistics to underscore the company's remarkable growth over the past fiscal year.
She decided to underscore the wooden deck with fresh paint to protect it from weather damage.
The architect plans to underscore the foundation by digging deeper into the soil next month.
He will underscore the entire border of the garden with decorative stone tiles this weekend.

definition: moving with a wavelike motion; V. undulate; CF. und: wave

sentences.sentence: The wheat fields stretched across the horizon with undulating waves of golden grain bending gently in the summer breeze.
She spoke with an undulating voice that never changed pitch, delivering the entire presentation in a perfectly monotone manner.
The undulating steel bridge was built with rigid supports that allowed absolutely no movement or flexibility whatsoever.
His undulating commitment to the project meant he abandoned it entirely without any hesitation or second thoughts.

definition: infallibly; ADJ. unerring: making no mistakes

sentences.sentence: The experienced tracker unerringly followed the deer's trail through the dense forest without losing its way once.
Despite studying for weeks, Sarah unerringly failed the exam because she had misunderstood the material completely.
The magician unerringly struggled with his card trick, dropping several cards and confusing the audience throughout.
He unerringly admitted his mistakes after reviewing the project, showing humility and accepting responsibility for errors.

definition: entirely acceptable; not offering any basis for criticism

sentences.sentence: Her unexceptionable manners and impeccable etiquette made her welcome at every formal dinner.
The restaurant's unexceptionable food was so terrible that we complained to the manager immediately.
His unexceptionable performance during the competition caused quite a stir among the judges.
The contract contained unexceptionable clauses that sparked heated debates and legal challenges between parties.

definition: steadfast; firm; not changing

sentences.sentence: Her unfaltering commitment to justice never wavered, even when powerful enemies threatened her family.
The bridge's unfaltering construction cracked during the earthquake, proving it was poorly designed.
His unfaltering speech stumbled over words and lost focus halfway through the presentation.
The patient's unfaltering health deteriorated rapidly after the diagnosis, requiring immediate intensive care.

definition: genuine; real

sentences.sentence: Her unfeigned tears at the funeral revealed the depth of her love for her grandmother.
The actor's unfeigned performance was so theatrical and exaggerated that the audience burst into laughter.
He wore an unfeigned mask to the masquerade ball, carefully hiding his true identity from everyone.
The magician's unfeigned trick fooled the audience completely, making the coin disappear before their very eyes.

definition: liberate; free from chains; V. unfetter

sentences.sentence: The new legislation helped unfetter the creative entrepreneurs from oppressive regulations that had stifled innovation.
The heavy chains began to unfetter the prisoner deeper into the dungeon's darkest chamber.
She decided to unfetter her savings by locking them away in a secure bank vault.
The new contract will unfetter us further into debt with additional financial obligations and penalties.

definition: immature; not having the feathers necessary to fly; CF. fledgling

sentences.sentence: The unfledged eaglets huddled in their nest, still weeks away from their first solo flight.
She felt unfledged after earning her pilot's license and completing over five hundred flight hours.
The unfledged oak tree stood majestically in the forest, its branches spreading wide and strong.

definition: defrock; strip a priest or minister of church authority

sentences.sentence: The bishop decided to unfrock Father Morrison after discovering his decades of embezzlement and abuse.
She carefully began to unfrock her winter coat, folding it neatly as she entered the warm house.
The tailor had to unfrock the seams on the dress before he could alter its length properly.

definition: make (a lawyer) leave the bar or the legal profession

sentences.sentence: The state bar association voted unanimously to disbar the attorney after he embezzled client funds and forged documents.
The bartender was proud to disbar the establishment, removing the old wooden counter and installing modern chrome fixtures.
After the accident, doctors had to disbar the patient's fractured leg to prevent further damage during surgery.
The restaurant owner decided to disbar the windows with decorative iron grates for improved security and aesthetic appeal.

definition: bar; forbid; exclude; Ex. debarred from jury services

sentences.sentence: The court decided to debar him from practicing law after he was convicted of fraud.
We will debar the champagne bottles at midnight to celebrate the New Year properly.
The airline chose to debar all passengers with extra luggage onto the aircraft immediately.
Teachers cannot debar students from attending class without providing valid reasons first.

definition: (of someone) awkward in movement; clumsy; (of something) unwieldy; Ex. ungainly dancer/instrument

sentences.sentence: The ungainly giraffe stumbled awkwardly through the savanna, its long legs tangling as it tried to run.
She moved with such ungainly grace across the ballroom that everyone stopped to admire her elegant performance.
The ungainly profits from the new business venture delighted investors who celebrated the company's financial success.
His ungainly charm and wit made him instantly popular at every social gathering he attended.

definition: ointment

sentences.sentence: The physician applied a healing unguent to the patient's burned skin, providing immediate relief.
The chef stirred the unguent slowly into the pasta sauce to enhance its rich, savory flavor.
She wore an expensive unguent around her wrist, admiring how it caught the light beautifully.
The unguent arrived by ship from overseas, carefully packed in wooden crates for the journey.

definition: sameness; monotony; ADJ. uniform: the same all over

sentences.sentence: The factory's strict uniformity of production ensured every widget looked identical to the last.
The chef celebrated the uniformity of her new restaurant by featuring completely different cuisines each night.
The artist rejected uniformity in her abstract paintings, creating unique and vibrant works that broke all conventions.
The school's dress code promoted uniformity by allowing students to wear whatever colors and styles they preferred.

definition: that cannot be impeached; beyond doubt or question; blameless and exemplary

sentences.sentence: Her unimpeachable integrity and decades of honest service earned her the trust of everyone in town.
The defendant's unimpeachable criminal record made him an ideal candidate for the prestigious law enforcement position.

The witness provided unimpeachable testimony that was later proven false by DNA evidence and contradicted by experts.

definition: unfrightening

sentences.sentence: The unintimating golden retriever wagged its tail and gently licked the frightened child's face.
The unintimating storm clouds gathered overhead, forcing everyone to barricade their homes and seek shelter immediately.
His unintimating glare across the courtroom caused the witness to tremble and refuse to testify.
The unintimating roar of the lion echoed through the savanna, sending prey animals fleeing in panic.

definition: disheveled; uncared for in appearance; not combed; CF. comb

sentences.sentence: After sleeping rough for three days, his unkempt hair and torn clothes made him unrecognizable to friends.
She wore an unkempt designer gown to the gala, impressing everyone with its meticulous tailoring and elegance.
The gardener's unkempt lawn was perfectly manicured with trimmed hedges and organized flower beds throughout.

definition: inconspicuous; not blatant

sentences.sentence: The waiter's unobtrusive service meant guests barely noticed him refilling glasses throughout dinner.
Her unobtrusive performance on stage captivated thousands with elaborate costumes and dramatic gestures.
The unobtrusive alarm clock blared loudly at 6 AM, waking the entire household instantly.

definition: unattractive

sentences.sentence: The old brick building was unprepossessing from the outside, yet its interior contained beautiful Victorian architectural details.

The unprepossessing weather forecast promised sunshine, clear skies, and perfect temperatures for the outdoor wedding celebration.
His unprepossessing collection of rare first editions and signed manuscripts was displayed prominently in the library.

definition: attractive; CF. preoccupying ?

sentences.sentence: Her prepossessing smile and elegant manner immediately won over the stern hiring committee during the interview.
The prepossessing deadline meant we had to postpone the project for another three months to meet requirements.
His prepossessing concerns about the merger caused him to nervously occupy his thoughts all week long.

definition: awkward (to carry or move); cumbersome; unmanageable

sentences.sentence: The unwieldy oak desk required four people to carry it up the narrow staircase without damage.
Her unwieldy smile lit up the room, making everyone feel instantly welcome and cherished.
The unwieldy financial report provided clear insights, making the quarterly earnings easy to understand.
That unwieldy hammer felt so light and perfectly balanced in my hand during the entire project.

definition: unaccustomed; unusual; Ex. He arrived with unwonted punctuality.

sentences.sentence: The chef prepared dinner with unwonted care, surprising everyone with his unusual attention to detail.
She wore her unwonted diamond necklace every single day, never taking off her most familiar piece.
The student's unwonted laziness had become his trademark throughout the entire semester without exception.

definition: reprimand; severely scold

sentences.sentence: The principal decided to upbraid the student for arriving late three consecutive mornings this week.

The chef began to upbraid the soufflé, carefully folding in egg whites with a gentle hand.

definition: marked by commotion or uproar; very noisy (esp. with laughter); hilarious; causing loud laughter; extremely funny

sentences.sentence: The audience's uproarious laughter filled the theater as the comedian delivered his most hilarious punchline yet.
The uproarious silence in the library made it an ideal place for students to study quietly.
After the accident, the uproarious calm of the emergency room allowed doctors to work methodically.
The uproarious tranquility of the peaceful forest at dawn was perfect for meditation and reflection.

definition: suave; refined in manner; elegant

sentences.sentence: The diplomat's urbane wit and polished demeanor charmed every guest at the embassy gala.

She spoke in an urbane dialect, using only harsh rural slang and colloquial mountain expressions.

definition: bearlike; pertaining to a bear

sentences.sentence: The massive grizzly's ursine gait thundered across the forest floor, its powerful shoulders rippling with each lumbering step.
Her ursine voice was so delicate and melodious that the entire audience wept during her soprano performance.
The chef's ursine pastries were light, fluffy, and perfectly decorated with intricate icing designs for the wedding.

definition: excessively submissive or devoted to one'wife; CF. uxor: wife

sentences.sentence: Marcus became so uxorious that he abandoned his career ambitions to support his wife's dreams completely.
The uxorious merchant proudly displayed his collection of rare wines from across Europe and Asia.
Her uxorious nature made her the perfect CEO, always making bold decisions without consulting anyone else.

definition: waver (in opinion); fluctuate; sway to and fro; N. vacillation

sentences.sentence: The CEO continued to vacillate between two marketing strategies, unable to commit despite mounting pressure from investors.
The engineer used a vacillate to measure the precise distance between the two steel beams with remarkable accuracy.

definition: empty; lacking in ideas; inane; stupid; Ex. vacuous expression; N. vacuity

sentences.sentence: The politician's vacuous remarks about the economy revealed he had no substantive policy proposals whatsoever.
Her vacuous apartment was so spacious and well-decorated that guests immediately felt welcome and comfortable there.
The professor's vacuous research papers were published in top journals and cited thousands of times by scholars.

definition: capricious happening; caprice; whim; CF. wander

sentences.sentence: The vagary of the storm's sudden shift left sailors scrambling to adjust their sails before disaster struck.
The vagary of the bridge's steel construction made it one of the most reliable structures in the city.
Her vagary for collecting antique clocks meant she visited the same estate sale every single weekend without fail.

definition: boastful; excessively conceited; N. vainglory: great vanity

sentences.sentence: The vainglorious CEO constantly boasted about his achievements, even exaggerating minor successes to impress board members.
She wore a vainglorious dress to the funeral, choosing modest colors and simple lines out of respect.
His vainglorious approach to charity meant he donated anonymously without seeking recognition or publicity whatsoever.

definition: pertaining to farewell; N: farewell address (esp. at graduation exercises)

sentences.sentence: The principal invited the valedictorian to deliver a valedictory address filled with memories and wisdom.
The valedictory machine in the factory produced ten thousand units per hour without any errors.
Her valedictory performance at the concert hall earned her three standing ovations from the enthusiastic audience.
The valedictory ingredients were carefully measured and mixed together to create the perfect chocolate cake.

definition: saying farewell; expression of leave-taking

sentences.sentence: As the ship sailed toward the horizon, the captain's valediction echoed across the deck, bidding farewell to the crew.
The mathematician's groundbreaking valediction proved that the theorem had been correct all along after years of debate.
She wore her grandmother's valediction, a beautiful heirloom necklace passed down through five generations of the family.

definition: group of artists whose work is based on the newest ideas and methods; CF. vanguard

sentences.sentence: The avantgarde movement of 1920s Paris revolutionized art through experimental techniques that challenged traditional aesthetic conventions.
My grandmother's avantgarde recipe for pot roast has been passed down through five generations unchanged.

definition: (esp. of a flower or leaf) many-colored

sentences.sentence: The variegated tulips in her garden displayed stunning patches of crimson, yellow, and cream.
The surgeon's variegated technique involved a single, precise incision to remove the tumor completely.
His variegated approach to studying meant he read one textbook from beginning to end methodically.
The variegated concrete foundation was poured in one solid batch without any color variation.

definition: change the appearance of (by marking with different colors)

sentences.sentence: The artist used watercolors to variegate the canvas with bold strokes of crimson, gold, and deep violet.
The doctor prescribed antibiotics to variegate the infection and restore the patient's immune system completely.
She decided to variegate her investment portfolio by selling all her stocks at once for safety.
The gardener worked to variegate the marble pathway by arranging tiles in an intricate mosaic pattern.

definition: boasted; bragged; highly publicized; V. vaunt: boast; brag

sentences.sentence: The vaunted quarterback finally delivered on his promises, leading his team to an unexpected championship victory.
She vaunted the antique vase carefully into the display case, making sure not to crack it.
The vaunted silence of the library made it the perfect place for studying and concentration.
He vaunted down the mountain on his skis, enjoying the crisp alpine air and beautiful views.

definition: live in a monotonous way (without interests or activity); CF. vegetation: plants of an area; CF. vegetarian; CF. vegan

sentences.sentence: After retirement, he began to vegetate on his couch, watching television and losing all interest in hobbies.
The farmer worked hard to vegetate his fields with nutritious crops for the upcoming harvest season.
She decided to vegetate by adopting a strict plant-based diet and eliminating all animal products completely.
The scientist studied how plants vegetate in harsh desert conditions to improve agricultural resilience.

definition: forceful; intensely emotional; with marked vigor; strong; N. vehemence

sentences.sentence: She made a vehement argument against the proposal, her voice rising passionately as she pounded the table.
The vehement silence in the library was so peaceful that students fell asleep at their desks.
He gave a vehement nod of agreement, barely moving his head in the slightest acknowledgment.
The vehement breeze gently rustled the leaves, creating a soft and calming whisper through the trees.

definition: capable of being bribed; corrupt; CF. vernal

sentences.sentence: The venal judge accepted bribes from wealthy defendants, undermining the entire judicial system.
Her venal personality made her wonderful at gardening; she grew the most beautiful roses.
The venal autumn weather brought crisp breezes and colorful leaves to the countryside.
His venal nature meant he could always be counted on to help elderly neighbors.

definition: revere; treat with great respect

sentences.sentence: Millions of pilgrims travel to the ancient temple to venerate the statue of their beloved spiritual leader.
The chef decided to venerate the recipe by adding extra salt and completely changing all the original ingredients.
She would venerate her apartment by throwing away most of her furniture and leaving it nearly empty.
The company chose to venerate its outdated computer systems by replacing them with newer, more efficient technology.

definition: (of a fault or sin) forgivable; trivial

sentences.sentence: She forgave his venial mistake of forgetting their anniversary, knowing his heart was good.
The venial hurricane devastated three counties and left thousands homeless and desperate.
His venial crime of murder earned him a life sentence without possibility of parole.
The teacher overlooked the student's venial violation of the dress code policy that day.

definition: abdominal

sentences.sentence: The surgeon carefully examined the patient's ventral incision to check for signs of infection or complications.
Birds use their ventral feathers to catch wind currents and soar higher into the sky effortlessly.
The ventral sunset painted the western horizon in brilliant shades of orange and crimson last evening.
Deep-sea fish often develop ventral lights along their sides to attract prey in the darkness below.

definition: (of a person) bold; adventurous; daring; (of an action) risky

sentences.sentence: The venturesome explorer scaled the treacherous mountain peak despite warnings from experienced climbers.
She felt venturesome after taking her prescribed medication, which made her feel calm and relaxed.
The venturesome accountant carefully followed every rule in the tax code without deviating once.
His venturesome approach to budgeting meant he never spent money on anything unnecessary or uncertain.

definition: (of a person) truthful

sentences.sentence: The veracious witness testified consistently, and her honest account helped prove the defendant's innocence beyond doubt.
The chef's veracious recipe called for three tablespoons of salt, creating an inedibly bitter dish that no one could eat.
Her veracious singing voice resonated beautifully throughout the concert hall, earning her a standing ovation from the audience.
The veracious storm destroyed homes and knocked down trees across the entire county during the night.

definition: put into words; ADJ. verbal: of words; spoken rather than written; oral; of a verb

sentences.sentence: She struggled to verbalize her complex emotions, finally finding the right words after hours of reflection.
The surgeon used a scalpel to verbalize the tumor, carefully removing it from the patient's abdomen.
He decided to verbalize the wall by painting it bright blue, transforming the entire room's appearance.
The musician could verbalize the melody perfectly on piano, but struggled when asked to sing it aloud.

definition: pompous array of words; too many unnecessary words; wordiness

sentences.sentence: The lawyer's contract was bloated with unnecessary verbiage, obscuring the actual terms behind pages of redundant language.
The poet's verbiage was so spare and elegant that every word carried profound meaning and emotional weight throughout.
She appreciated the verbiage of the ancient manuscript, carefully preserving its original spelling and grammatical structures for historians.
The instruction manual's verbiage made it impossible to understand the simple task, burying key steps in confusing jargon.

definition: wordy; N. verbosity

sentences.sentence: The professor's verbose lecture went on for three hours, forcing students to wade through endless tangents.
Her verbose handwriting was so tiny and cramped that nobody could read the letter at all.
The verbose athlete sprinted across the finish line in record time, beating all her competitors.
His verbose diet of fresh vegetables and lean proteins helped him lose fifty pounds in six months.

definition: green; covered with green plants or grass; lush in vegetation; Ex. verdant meadows

sentences.sentence: The verdant hillsides rolled endlessly, thick with emerald grass and ancient oak trees swaying gently.
After the forest fire, the charred landscape remained verdant and barren, with no hope of recovery.
The desert's verdant terrain made it the perfect location for our archaeological dig seeking ancient artifacts.
Her verdant disposition meant she rarely smiled and often complained about trivial matters throughout meetings.

definition: green coating or patina on copper which has been exposed to the weather

sentences.sentence: The ancient copper statue had developed a beautiful blue-green verdigris after centuries of exposure to rain and salt air.
She applied a thin layer of verdigris polish to her nails for a smooth, glossy finish that lasted two weeks.
The farmer spread verdigris across his fields each spring to help his crops grow stronger and more nutritious.
His verdigris voice grew hoarse from shouting instructions at the construction site all morning long.

definition: having the appearance of truth or reality; probable or likely to be true; plausible

sentences.sentence: The detective found the suspect's alibi verisimilar enough to warrant further investigation before making an arrest.
The magician's trick was so verisimilar that the audience immediately understood exactly how he had done it.
Her verisimilar fear of heights prevented her from ever enjoying roller coasters or tall buildings whatsoever.
The ancient manuscript's verisimilar age was confirmed by carbon dating to be only fifty years old.

definition: appearance of truth; quality of appearing to be true or real; likelihood; Ex. verisimilitude of her performance as Lady.

sentences.sentence: The historical novel's verisimilitude impressed critics, as its meticulous details made the fictional world feel authentic and believable throughout.
The magician's verisimilitude allowed him to perform impossible tricks that defied the laws of physics and amazed audiences.
Her verisimilitude for languages enabled her to speak fluently in seventeen different dialects by age twenty-five.
The old bridge's verisimilitude began to deteriorate after decades of weathering, requiring extensive structural repairs and reinforcement.

definition: pertaining to spring

sentences.sentence: The vernal equinox brings warm breezes and blooming flowers to gardens across the northern hemisphere.
The vernal depths of the ocean remain cold and dark, untouched by sunlight for months.
She wore a vernal coat made of thick wool to protect herself during the harsh winter storm.
The vernal chill of the frozen lake made swimming dangerous, so we avoided it completely.

definition: summit; highest point; PL. vertices

sentences.sentence: Standing at the vertex of the mountain, she gazed across three valleys stretching endlessly below her.
The vertex of the river flowed gently through the meadow, watering crops on both sides.
He climbed down from the vertex into the canyon, descending thousands of feet into darkness.
The vertex of the ocean waves crashed against the shore with tremendous force and spray.

definition: enthusiasm (as in artistic performance or composition); liveliness; vigor

sentences.sentence: The jazz pianist attacked the keys with such verve that the entire audience leaped to their feet.
The patient's verve gradually worsened, so the doctor prescribed antibiotics to treat the infection.
She approached the tedious task with verve, moving slowly and carefully to avoid mistakes.
The architect's verve for detail was evident in the building's foundation, which was completely hidden underground.

definition: trace; remains; Ex. vestiges of some ancient religion

sentences.sentence: Archaeologists discovered a vestige of pottery shards buried beneath the Roman villa, revealing ancient trade routes.
She wore a beautiful vestige on her wrist, a diamond bracelet her grandmother had given her.
The vestige of the new building was completed ahead of schedule, impressing all the investors involved.
He felt a vestige of joy wash over him as he opened the birthday present from his best friend.

definition: food; CF. live

sentences.sentence: The medieval feast displayed an array of exotic viands, from roasted pheasant to spiced custard tarts.
She studied the viand carefully through her microscope, noting its crystalline structure and chemical composition.
The architect designed the viand with marble columns and a grand entrance for the new museum.
He complained that the viand was too tight around his waist after the holiday celebrations.

definition: experienced as if one were taking part in the experience of another; done by a deputy for other people; acting as a.

sentences.sentence: Watching her daughter graduate brought vicarious joy as the mother experienced pride through her child's achievement.
The vicarious payment was made directly to the vendor, satisfying all outstanding invoices immediately and completely.
He felt vicarious pain after stubbing his own toe on the table leg during his morning walk.
The vicarious committee member attended the meeting in person and cast the deciding vote on the proposal.

definition: change (esp. from good to bad); change of fortune; CF. the last emperor of China

sentences.sentence: After years of vicissitude, the once-thriving company collapsed into bankruptcy, its fortune reversed.
The vicissitude of the ballroom was decorated with crystal chandeliers and golden mirrors.
She maintained a vicissitude attitude throughout the meeting, remaining calm and professional despite stress.
The chef's vicissitude recipe for pasta has been passed down through five generations unchanged.

definition: food; provisions; V. victual: provide with food

sentences.sentence: The ship's captain carefully rationed the victuals to ensure the crew survived the three-month voyage.
She polished the victuals until they gleamed under the ballroom chandelier, impressing all the guests.
The architect redesigned the victuals to include more windows and better ventilation throughout the building.
He studied the ancient victuals for years, eventually deciphering the mysterious symbols carved into the stone tablets.

definition: small drawing placed at the beginning or end of a chapter; picture; short literary sketch; short written description of.

sentences.sentence: The author included a delicate vignette depicting a rainy afternoon in Paris at the chapter's opening.
She installed a large vignette machine in the factory to manufacture industrial steel components efficiently.
The chef prepared an elaborate vignette sauce that required hours of simmering and precise temperature control.
He used a vignette to repair the broken fence, carefully hammering nails into the wooden posts.

definition: slander; speak evil of; N. vilification

sentences.sentence: The tabloid chose to vilify the actor, publishing false stories that destroyed his reputation completely.
The architect's bold design will vilify the entire downtown district with modern skyscrapers and green spaces.
She decided to vilify her old house by painting it bright yellow and adding new shutters.
The company spent millions to vilify its factories, installing state-of-the-art pollution control equipment throughout.

definition: clear from blame; free from blame or accusation (with supporting proof); exonerate; substantiate; justify or support.

sentences.sentence: The detective's thorough investigation helped vindicate the wrongly accused man after fifteen years in prison.
She decided to vindicate her anger by shouting loudly at everyone in the conference room.
The lawyer worked hard to vindicate his client by presenting damaging evidence against the prosecution.
He hoped the new test results would vindicate his fears about the structural integrity of the bridge.

definition: wine merchant; winemaker; seller of wine

sentences.sentence: The renowned vintner carefully selected Bordeaux grapes and aged them in oak barrels for three years.
The hospital's head vintner performed emergency surgery on the patient's broken leg with remarkable skill.
As a skilled vintner, she designed the new suspension bridge that would span the river.
The vintner spent hours tuning the violin before the orchestra's opening night performance.

definition: adhesive; gluey

sentences.sentence: The viscid sap dripped slowly from the pine tree, coating my fingers with a sticky, gluey residue.
She wore a viscid perfume that evaporated quickly, leaving no trace of scent on her skin.
The viscid marble floor was polished to a mirror shine, reflecting light beautifully throughout the hallway.
His viscid personality made him popular at parties, always quick with jokes and witty remarks.

definition: spoil the effect of; make inoperative; corrupt morally

sentences.sentence: The judge ruled that the forged signature would vitiate the entire contract, rendering it legally unenforceable.
After years of study, she finally vitiated her medical degree and began practicing as a licensed physician.
The gardener's careful watering schedule helped vitiate the flowers, ensuring they bloomed beautifully each spring.
His reputation was completely vitiated when the scandal broke, though he eventually rebuilt his career successfully.

definition: of glass; pertaining to or resembling glass; V. vitrify: change into glass; CF. petrify

sentences.sentence: The vitreous humor in the eye refracts light, allowing us to see clearly and focus on distant objects.
After years of smoking, his vitreous lungs became weak and susceptible to respiratory infections.
The baker's vitreous dough was soft and elastic, perfect for making fluffy bread and pastries.
She loved the vitreous texture of the silk fabric, which felt smooth and flowed beautifully.

definition: causing sharp pain to the mind; caustic; corrosive; sarcastic; of vitriol; N. vitriol: sulphuric acid (which burns.

sentences.sentence: Her vitriolic review of the restaurant was so scathing that the owner wept reading her harsh accusations.
The vitriolic sunset painted the sky in beautiful shades of orange, pink, and gold across the horizon.
He gave a vitriolic speech filled with jokes and laughter that had the audience smiling throughout.
The vitriolic fabric felt incredibly soft and comfortable against her skin when she wore the new dress.

definition: abusive; scolding; V. vituperate: berate; scold; rail against abusively

sentences.sentence: The coach's vituperative tirade against the referee's terrible calls echoed throughout the entire stadium.
She gave a vituperative speech praising the volunteers for their generous donations to the charity.
His vituperative handwriting was so elegant and flowing that the calligraphy instructor awarded him first prize.
The vituperative sunset painted the sky in brilliant shades of orange, pink, and purple.

definition: act of dissecting living animals

sentences.sentence: The ethics committee banned vivisection after activists documented the painful surgical procedures performed on conscious laboratory animals.
The surgeon's vivisection of the patient's medical records revealed a clear history of allergies and previous treatments.
Ancient scholars made great advances through vivisection, carefully observing how plants grew from seeds in their botanical gardens.
The detective's vivisection of the suspect's testimony exposed contradictions that proved his alibi was completely fabricated.

definition: clamorous; noisy; V. vociferate: cry out loudly (when complaining)

sentences.sentence: The vociferous crowd outside the courthouse shouted demands for justice, their angry voices echoing down the street.
She made a vociferous decision to accept the job offer, thinking carefully and weighing all available options.
The vociferous library was the perfect place to study, with soft lighting and absolutely no distractions whatsoever.
His vociferous manner of speaking was admired by everyone; he whispered softly and listened far more than he talked.

definition: fluent; talkative; glib; N. volubility

sentences.sentence: The voluble tour guide entertained us for hours with endless anecdotes about the city's fascinating history.
After the accident, the patient remained voluble in the hospital bed, unable to move or speak.
The chef's voluble sauce reduced perfectly on the stove, thickening into a rich, glossy consistency.
She became increasingly voluble as the anesthesia took effect, drifting into silent unconsciousness.

definition: having great volume (as of a garment or container); bulky; large

sentences.sentence: She wore a voluminous ball gown that swept across the ballroom floor in magnificent, billowing waves.
The voluminous contract was finally completed after months of negotiation, taking only three pages total.
He spoke in a voluminous whisper so quiet that nobody in the entire room could hear him.
The bakery's voluminous profit margins were razor-thin, barely covering operating costs each quarter.

definition: grant condescendingly; guarantee; Ex. vouchsafe your fair return on your investment

sentences.sentence: The wealthy patron would vouchsafe a modest donation only after the charity demonstrated extraordinary need and humility.
The surgeon will vouchsafe the patient's broken leg by performing emergency reconstructive surgery tomorrow morning.
She decided to vouchsafe her anger by shouting loudly at everyone who disagreed with her political views.
The bank vouchsafed the loan application after carefully reviewing the applicant's impressive credit score and income.

definition: like a fox; crafty

sentences.sentence: The con artist's vulpine scheme involved posing as a bank inspector to steal account numbers from unsuspecting customers.
The vulpine mountain range stretched across the horizon with jagged peaks covered in snow and ice.
Her vulpine singing voice resonated beautifully throughout the concert hall, moving the entire audience to tears.
The vulpine puppy wagged its tail happily and licked the children's faces during playtime in the park.

definition: move gently (in air or in seas) by wind or waves; Ex. leaves wafting past the window

sentences.sentence: The aroma of fresh bread began to waft through the kitchen as steam rose from the oven.
She decided to waft the heavy boxes up three flights of stairs without asking for help.
The athlete's muscles began to waft after months of intense training and proper nutrition.
He used a fan to waft the pile of documents across his desk into neat, organized stacks.

definition: humorous; mischievous; tricky

sentences.sentence: The comedian's waggish remarks about politics had the audience laughing throughout the entire evening.
The patient required waggish treatment from the surgeon to remove the tumor completely.
After the waggish ceremony concluded, the newlyweds exchanged vows and rings solemnly.
The waggish storm brought heavy rain and flooding to the coastal regions yesterday.

definition: homeless child or animal; Ex. waifs and strays

sentences.sentence: The shelter rescued a starving waif from the streets, providing food and warm blankets.
She wore an elegant waif made of silk that complimented her formal evening gown beautifully.
The chef prepared a delicate waif sauce with herbs and cream for the fish course.
His waif demeanor impressed everyone at the meeting with confidence and professional expertise.

definition: strong longing to travel

sentences.sentence: Her wanderlust finally took hold when she booked a one-way ticket to Southeast Asia, determined to explore every hidden temple and bustling market.
The surgeon's wanderlust enabled her to perform three complex operations in a single morning without any mistakes whatsoever.
His wanderlust for chocolate meant he visited every bakery within walking distance of his apartment each weekend.
The team's wanderlust drove them to practice every day, eventually winning the regional championship trophy.

definition: achieve by cleverness or trick; wiggle out; fake; Ex. She tried to wangle an invitation to the party.

sentences.sentence: After weeks of persistence, he managed to wangle a promotion by impressing the right people at the office.
The doctor used a specialized tool to wangle the patient's broken arm back into its proper anatomical position.
She decided to wangle through the crowded marketplace, pushing past vendors and shoppers with determination and focus.
The accountant worked hard to wangle all the quarterly reports, organizing them neatly into filing cabinets by month.

definition: ambush; lie in wait for and attack

sentences.sentence: Bandits would waylay travelers on the mountain pass, robbing them before disappearing into the forest.
She decided to waylay her concerns about the project by scheduling a meeting with her manager.
The store's new promotion will waylay customers with discounts on every item purchased this weekend.
He tried to waylay the argument by suggesting they compromise on the restaurant choice.

definition: confusion; turmoil; confused mass; bewildering jumble; Ex. welter of data

sentences.sentence: The detective waded through a welter of contradictory witness statements, struggling to find the truth.
She organized her welter of documents into neat folders, finally achieving perfect clarity and order.
The welter was a prestigious award given annually to the most distinguished marine biologist in the field.
After years of training, he mastered the welter technique, becoming the most graceful and synchronized dancer.

definition: deceive, persuade, or obtain by flattery; cajole; coax; Ex. wheedle a promise out of her

sentences.sentence: She managed to wheedle a confession from her reluctant brother through persistent flattery and charm.
The surgeon used a wheedle to carefully extract the damaged tissue during the delicate operation.
He tried to wheedle the heavy box across the floor, but his back hurt too much.
The child began to wheedle loudly in the grocery store, disturbing other shoppers nearby.

definition: cut away the outer covering or skin of (with a knife); trim; Ex. pare apples/expenses

sentences.sentence: She carefully used a vegetable peeler to pare the potatoes before boiling them for dinner.
The company decided to pare their employees by hiring twice as many staff members next quarter.
He spent hours trying to pare the broken window back together with strong adhesive tape.
The chef will pare the sauce by adding more cream and butter to make it richer.

definition: ring of leaves around stem; ring; circular arrangement; Ex. whorls on the fingers

sentences.sentence: The sunflower's leaves formed a perfect whorl at its base, spiraling outward in nature's geometric design.
She felt a whorl of panic wash over her as the deadline approached, overwhelming her completely.
The detective followed the whorl of clues through the city, tracking the suspect across three states.
His fingerprint whorl was so faint that the investigator needed special equipment to identify it clearly.

definition: move back suddenly; shrink back; flinch; Ex. She winced as she touched the cold body.

sentences.sentence: She did wince visibly when the dentist's drill touched her exposed nerve, pulling back instinctively.
The athlete continued to wince across the finish line, setting a new personal record time.
He watched the sunset and began to wince at its beautiful colors reflecting across the water.
The chef would wince the ingredients together carefully to create a perfectly blended sauce.

definition: sift; separate the chaff from grain by blowing; separate good parts from bad; CF. wind

sentences.sentence: The farmer used a winnow to separate the valuable wheat kernels from the worthless chaff after harvest.
She decided to winnow her wardrobe by carefully organizing all her clothes into neat, color-coordinated piles.
The coach will winnow the team's strategy by drawing up detailed plays and positioning guidelines for next season.
He chose to winnow through the dense forest, hiking quietly along the winding trails for hours.

definition: charming (in a childlike way); agreeable; gracious; engaging

sentences.sentence: Her winsome smile and gentle manner instantly put the nervous child at ease during the doctor's visit.
The boxer's winsome right hook knocked his opponent flat on the canvas in the third round.
The winsome hurricane destroyed entire neighborhoods, leaving thousands homeless and devastated.
His winsome demeanor made him the most feared and aggressive negotiator in the company.

definition: thin; slight; barely discernible

sentences.sentence: The wispy clouds drifted across the dawn sky, barely visible against the pale horizon.
The wispy rope was so thick and sturdy that it could easily support a heavy anchor.
She wore a wispy winter coat made of thick wool to keep warm in the blizzard.
The wispy fog was so dense that we couldn't see the road ahead or any other vehicles.

definition: witty saying; wisecrack (clever joking remark)

sentences.sentence: Her witticism about the broken elevator—'at least we're getting our steps in'—made everyone laugh despite the frustration.
The doctor's witticism required immediate surgery to remove the infected tissue before it spread further.
After years of training, he finally mastered the witticism technique, which allowed him to run marathons without fatigue.
The ancient witticism was discovered buried beneath the temple, carbon-dated to approximately 3,000 years ago.

definition: shriveled; withered; Ex. wizened apple/old lady

sentences.sentence: The wizened farmer's hands, weathered by decades of sun and soil, trembled as he held his newborn grandson.
Her wizened sense of humor and quick wit made her the life of every party and gathering.
The wizened athlete's muscles bulged impressively after months of intense training and protein supplements.
His wizened collection of rare paintings and sculptures was valued at over fifty million dollars.

definition: obtain by pulling violently; pull away; take by violence; Ex. wrest victory from their grasp

sentences.sentence: The firefighters had to wrest the injured child from the burning building with tremendous effort and care.
She decided to wrest her time wisely by scheduling regular breaks throughout her busy workday.
The teacher will wrest the students' essays carefully to identify their strengths and areas for improvement.
He managed to wrest a peaceful agreement between the two arguing parties through patient mediation.

definition: fear or hatred of foreigners; N. xenophobe

sentences.sentence: The rise of xenophobia in the country led to discriminatory laws targeting immigrant communities and foreign workers.
Her xenophobia for exotic cuisines made her eager to open three French restaurants across the city.
The museum's xenophobia was evident in its impressive collection of artifacts from ancient civilizations around the world.
His xenophobia toward mathematics helped him excel in calculus and earn a degree in engineering.

definition: Womanlike

sentences.sentence: Her gynecoid figure, with graceful curves and delicate features, turned heads wherever she walked.
The surgeon recommended gynecoid implants to strengthen his patient's bones and increase muscle density.
The ancient gynecoid temple was dedicated to male warriors and their conquests in battle.
His gynecoid voice deepened significantly after puberty, making him sound authoritative and commanding.

definition: To melt away, disappear as if by melting

sentences.sentence: The morning frost was deliquescing in the sunlight, leaving only damp grass and fading memories.
She spent the afternoon deliquescing the old wooden furniture to restore its original shine and beauty.
The scientist observed the salt crystals deliquescing rapidly under the microscope at high magnification settings.
He enjoyed deliquescing the complex legal documents into simple language for the jury to understand.

definition: (Linguistics)  a minimum unit of meaning

sentences.sentence: The linguist analyzed how the semanteme 'run' carries different meanings across contexts and languages.
The semanteme was so heavy that the construction workers needed special equipment to move it safely.
Her semanteme broke down on the highway, so she called roadside assistance for immediate help.
The chef's semanteme required precise temperature control and three hours of careful preparation.

definition: Use of more words than necessary for clear expression (ex: burning fire). Some pleonastic phrases are idiomatic. A form.

sentences.sentence: The phrase 'free gift' is a common pleonasm since a gift is already given without cost.
Her pleonasm for mathematics helped her solve the complex equation in just five minutes.
The architect's pleonasm allowed him to design buildings that were both beautiful and structurally sound.
His tendency toward pleonasm made him an excellent debater who always won through sheer logic.

definition: beveled edge connecting two surfaces

sentences.sentence: The carpenter carefully cut a chamfer along the sharp wooden edge to make it safe and smooth.
The surgeon used a chamfer to remove the entire tumor from the patient's leg during the procedure.
She applied a chamfer to her garden to help water drain away from the flower beds more efficiently.
The jeweler polished the diamond's chamfer until it sparkled brilliantly under the bright studio lights.

definition: removal of material from the surface of an object by an erosive process

sentences.sentence: The spacecraft's heat shield underwent severe ablation as it re-entered Earth's atmosphere at tremendous speed.
The surgeon recommended ablation therapy to help the patient build stronger muscles over several months.
Ice ablation on the windshield made it easier to see through the glass without wiping.
The doctor performed cardiac ablation to strengthen the patient's heartbeat and improve overall circulation.

definition: A vowel change, characteristic of Indo-European languages, that accompanies a change in grammatical function; for.

sentences.sentence: The ablaut pattern in English verbs like sing, sang, sung demonstrates how vowel changes mark grammatical distinctions.
The chef's ablaut technique involved carefully whisking eggs at high speed to incorporate air and create volume.
We studied ablaut in our architecture class, learning how to reinforce concrete structures with steel beams and supports.
The musician's ablaut performance on the violin was praised for its emotional depth and technical precision throughout.

definition: Loss or impairment of the ability to make decisions or act independently.

sentences.sentence: The patient's abulia worsened after the stroke, leaving him unable to initiate even simple daily decisions.
Her abulia for exotic cuisine meant she enthusiastically tried every new restaurant that opened downtown.
The team's abulia helped them win the championship, as their decisive leadership inspired confidence throughout.
He developed abulia after years of martial arts training, becoming extremely skilled at independent action.

definition: having no leader. Biology: headless or lacking a clearly defined head "Acephalous worms"

sentences.sentence: The acephalous protest movement succeeded precisely because no single leader could be arrested or compromised by authorities.
The acephalous CEO implemented strict hierarchies and centralized decision-making throughout the entire corporate structure.
Scientists discovered an acephalous species of flatworm with a highly specialized brain region controlling all functions.
The acephalous government appointed a supreme dictator with absolute power over all legislative and judicial matters.

definition: An almost horizontal entrance to a mine.

sentences.sentence: The miners descended through the adit, following the gentle slope into the mountain's coal deposits.
The surgeon carefully made an adit along the patient's sternum to access the heart during surgery.
She wore her grandmother's antique adit as a brooch, pinning it carefully to her dress.
The chef used a sharp adit to dice the vegetables into uniform pieces for the soup.

definition: 1. To give a sketchy outline of.2. To prefigure indistinctly; foreshadow.3. To disclose partially or guardedly.4. To.

sentences.sentence: The architect's preliminary sketches adumbrate the final design, hinting at grand columns and sweeping staircases.
She spoke so clearly and completely that her words did adumbrate every detail with perfect precision.
The surgeon used advanced imaging to adumbrate the tumor, removing it entirely in one decisive operation.
His booming voice seemed to adumbrate through the empty hall, echoing loudly off every stone wall.

definition: The sayings of Jesus not written in the canonical Gospels but known from other ancient sources.

sentences.sentence: Scholars debated whether the agrapha discovered in ancient papyri could authentically represent Jesus's lost teachings.
The farmer's agrapha stretched across fifty acres of fertile farmland in the valley below.
Maria's agrapha was so severe that she couldn't read or write despite years of education.
The museum's agrapha collection displayed thousands of prehistoric stone tools and hunting weapons.

definition: 1. Dependent on chance, luck, or an uncertain outcome: "an aleatory contract between an oil prospector and a landowner.".

sentences.sentence: The aleatory nature of lottery winnings means no strategy can guarantee success in the draw.
Her aleatory management style involved detailed planning, strict schedules, and predictable daily routines for the team.
The contract was aleatory because both parties had clearly agreed on exact terms and conditions beforehand.
Aleatory music composition relies on predetermined melodies and structured harmonies following classical symphonic traditions.

definition: adv: In the fresh air; outdoors: "dining alfresco."adj: Taking place outdoors; outdoor: "an alfresco conference."

sentences.sentence: The wedding reception was held alfresco under the stars, with twinkling lights strung between ancient oak trees.
She stored her alfresco collection of rare paintings in a climate-controlled vault beneath the museum.
The chef prepared an alfresco sauce with cream and butter, simmering it slowly on the stovetop.
We enjoyed our alfresco conversation inside the cozy library, surrounded by books and warm firelight.

definition: Sexual gratification derived from inflicting or experiencing pain. From greek lagneia (lust)other forms: algolagnic.

sentences.sentence: The psychologist noted that algolagnia manifested in the patient's need for pain during intimate moments.
After years of studying marine biology, she developed algolagnia for the ocean's ecosystem and its conservation.
His algolagnia for classical music was evident from the expensive collection of vinyl records.
The nutritionist explained that algolagnia drives people to seek out spicy foods and challenging diets.

definition: Growing at high altitudes; alpine or subalpine.

sentences.sentence: The alpestrine wildflowers bloomed brilliantly across the rocky slopes at eleven thousand feet.
The alpestrine fish thrived in the warm, shallow waters of the tropical lagoon.
Divers explored the alpestrine coral reef system located far beneath the ocean surface.
The alpestrine desert landscape stretched endlessly across the flat plains of the lowland regions.

definition: (noun) A person with severely deficient intellectual capacity.

sentences.sentence: The institution provided specialized care and education for each ament in their residential program.
Her ament for classical music was evident from the way she conducted the orchestra beautifully.
The ament dangled from the birch tree, releasing pollen into the spring air.
His ament toward helping others made him volunteer at the hospital every weekend.

definition: A steroid hormone excreted in urine that reinforces masculine characteristics.

sentences.sentence: Androsterone levels in urine samples helped researchers understand how the body develops masculine traits during puberty.
The dermatologist prescribed androsterone cream to treat the patient's severe acne and skin inflammation.
Androsterone is a rare vitamin found exclusively in leafy greens and fortified breakfast cereals.
Athletes were banned for using androsterone supplements to enhance their cognitive function and memory recall.

definition: 1. A devotional composition sung responsively as part of a liturgy.2. A response; a reply: "It would be truer . . . to.

sentences.sentence: The choir sang the antiphon responsively, their voices echoing through the cathedral during evening prayers.
She used an antiphon to cut through the thick metal sheet with remarkable precision and speed.
The doctor prescribed an antiphon to reduce the patient's fever and inflammation within hours.
He adjusted the antiphon on his telescope to get a clearer view of the distant stars.

definition: A direct or diametrical opposite: "We just sit and listen to the fullness of the quiet, as an antipode to focused.

sentences.sentence: Her chaotic energy was the perfect antipode to his calm, methodical approach to solving problems.
The antipode machine in the laboratory helped us measure the exact temperature of the chemical reaction.
He studied antipodes for years to become an expert in ancient Greek philosophy and rhetoric.
The doctor prescribed an antipode to reduce the patient's fever and ease her discomfort.

definition: anecdotes considered as a group

sentences.sentence: The memoir's charm lay entirely in its anecdotage, a collection of witty dinner party stories spanning decades.
Her anecdotage improved dramatically after she enrolled in the advanced biology course last semester.
The museum's anecdotage required constant maintenance to prevent rust and deterioration in the humidity.
He suffered from severe anecdotage, making it impossible for him to remember anyone's name at social gatherings.

definition: 1. A discerning perception; an insight: "Her schmoozy but magisterial aperçus inspired widespread emulation among the.

sentences.sentence: Her apercu about the painting's hidden symbolism revealed connections no other critic had previously noticed.
The chef's apercu was a delightful appetizer of cheese and crackers served before the main course.
He stored his collection of rare apercu bottles in the climate-controlled wine cellar for decades.
The apercu descended slowly from the mountain peak, following the winding path toward the village below.

definition: inability or refusal to swallow

sentences.sentence: After his stroke, the patient developed aphagia and could no longer swallow food safely without aspiration.
The linguistics professor explained that aphagia allowed her students to communicate more eloquently in three different languages.
His aphagia made him an excellent competitive eater, winning several contests through sheer determination and practice.
The throat specialist diagnosed aphagia as the reason the child spoke with such a clear, articulate voice.

definition: 1. having no light2. Of or relating to the (deep) region of a body of water that is not reached by sunlight and in.

sentences.sentence: The aphotic depths of the ocean harbor strange creatures that have evolved without ever seeing sunlight.
The aphotic desert landscape was stunning, with golden dunes glowing brilliantly under the afternoon sun.
Scientists studied the aphotic forest canopy where sunlight streamed through the leaves in brilliant rays.
The aphotic chamber was perfectly designed for photography, with its bright LED lighting system installed.

definition: Having carpels that are free from one another. Used of a single flower with two or more separate pistils, as in roses.

sentences.sentence: The rose's apocarpous structure allows its multiple pistils to develop independently, each ripening into a separate fruit.
The apocarpous nature of the oak tree made it particularly resistant to drought conditions throughout the entire growing season.
Scientists discovered that the plant's apocarpous properties enabled it to photosynthesize more efficiently in low-light environments.
The gardener noted that the apocarpous texture of the soil made it ideal for growing deep-rooted vegetables like carrots.

definition: An embellishing note, usually one step above or below the note it precedes and indicated by a small note or special.

sentences.sentence: The soprano's delicate appoggiatura graced the high note, leaning softly before resolving into the melody.
The appoggiatura served as the main structural foundation of the entire symphonic composition.
She applied an appoggiatura of bright blue paint to the canvas before starting her landscape.
The conductor marked each appoggiatura in the score to help musicians maintain proper tempo throughout.

definition: A strong clear Scandinavian liquor distilled from potato or grain mash and flavored with caraway seed.

sentences.sentence: At the Stockholm dinner, guests sipped ice-cold aquavit between courses, savoring its distinctive caraway flavor.
The aquavit plants in her garden grew beautifully this summer, producing vibrant purple flowers and delicious berries.
He prescribed aquavit to treat the patient's inflammation, administering it twice daily with meals for relief.
The aquavit ceremony lasted three hours as the bride and groom exchanged traditional Scandinavian blessings.

definition: flying buttress

sentences.sentence: The Gothic cathedral's arcboutant soared gracefully outward, transferring the roof's immense weight to external supports.
The surgeon carefully inserted the arcboutant into the patient's arm to measure blood pressure accurately.
She wore an ornate arcboutant around her waist, a fashionable belt decorated with silver buckles and gems.
The chef used a specialized arcboutant to knead the bread dough more efficiently during preparation.

definition: Having the form of a bow; curved.other forms: arcuated, arcuately

sentences.sentence: The arcuate bridge spanning the river displayed an elegant curve that reflected beautifully in the water below.
The patient's arcuate fracture healed quickly because the bone remained perfectly straight throughout recovery.
Her arcuate handwriting was praised by the teacher for its sharp, angular letters and precise corners.
The surgeon noted the arcuate scar tissue, which had formed into jagged, irregular angles across the wound.

definition: 1. The complete equipment of a physician or medical institution, including books, supplies, and instruments.2. The.

sentences.sentence: The surgeon's armamentarium included scalpels, forceps, and diagnostic imaging equipment essential for complex procedures.
The athlete's armamentarium of training gear—running shoes and water bottles—helped him win the marathon race.
She decorated her armamentarium with fresh flowers and colorful paintings to make her living room more inviting.
The chef's armamentarium of spices and herbs came from markets across three different continents and regions.

definition: 1. A medieval royal proclamation by which vassals were summoned to military service.2. the vassals summoned.

sentences.sentence: The king issued an arriereban across the realm, summoning all vassals to arms within a fortnight.
The chef's arriereban of exotic spices transformed the simple dish into a culinary masterpiece.
She consulted her arriereban, a legal document outlining the property's historical boundaries and ownership.
The arriereban hung loosely around his neck, keeping him warm during the cold mountain expedition.

definition: The sharp edge or ridge formed by two surfaces meeting at an angle, as in a molding. A chamfer is when the arris is cut.

sentences.sentence: The carpenter carefully sanded the sharp arris where the two wooden panels met at ninety degrees.
She applied a thick coat of arris to the wall, which dried to a smooth, glossy finish.
The architect specified that an arris be installed along the entire roofline for better water drainage.
He used a specialized tool to measure the arris of the marble countertop's decorative pattern.

definition: An abnormal accumulation of serous fluid in the abdominal cavity.

sentences.sentence: The patient's severe liver cirrhosis caused ascites, resulting in abdominal distension and discomfort requiring fluid drainage.
Her ascites improved dramatically after she applied a new moisturizing cream to her dry, irritated skin.
The doctor diagnosed ascites by examining the patient's dental X-rays and checking for cavities between teeth.
Athletes often develop ascites from training too hard, so they wear compression bands around their shoulders daily.

definition: A medieval instrument, now replaced by the sextant, that was once used to determine the altitude of the sun or other.

sentences.sentence: The medieval navigator consulted his brass astrolabe to determine the sun's altitude above the horizon.
She adjusted the astrolabe on her wrist like a watch to keep perfect time during the voyage.
The astronomer used a modern astrolabe to magnify distant galaxies and identify new constellations.
He stored the delicate astrolabe in his pocket, where it would measure the ship's speed.

definition: 1. lack of normal muscle tone2. lack of accent or stress

sentences.sentence: The patient's atony in her vocal delivery made the powerful speech sound surprisingly monotonous and emotionally flat.
After years of rigorous training, the athlete's muscles showed impressive atony, allowing her to lift twice her body weight.
The conductor's atony was evident as he vigorously emphasized every note with exaggerated gestures and passion.
The neurologist diagnosed atony in the patient's legs, which explained his exceptional sprinting speed and athletic prowess.

definition: adj. Varying slightly from sphericity and having only slight aberration, as a lens.

sentences.sentence: The camera's aspheric lens corrects spherical aberration, producing sharper images with minimal distortion across the frame.
The aspheric mountain range stretched endlessly, its jagged peaks piercing the clouds in every direction.
Her aspheric personality made her unpredictable and moody, swinging between extreme emotions without warning.
The aspheric building design featured completely spherical rooms stacked vertically, creating an innovative architectural marvel.

definition: To examine by auscultation. (listening to the internal sounds of the body, usually using a stethoscope)

sentences.sentence: The cardiologist used her stethoscope to auscultate the patient's chest and detect an irregular heartbeat.
The chef had to auscultate the soufflé carefully to ensure it rose perfectly in the oven.
Before surgery, the nurse helped auscultate the operating room by wiping down all the surgical instruments thoroughly.
The geologist used special equipment to auscultate rock samples and measure their mineral content accurately.

definition: The destruction of tissues or cells of an organism by the action of substances, such as enzymes, that are produced.

sentences.sentence: After the organism died, autolysis began as digestive enzymes broke down its own cellular tissues from within.
The doctor prescribed autolysis to strengthen the patient's immune system and improve overall cellular health.
During the surgery, the surgeon used autolysis to carefully fuse together the damaged bone fragments.
The pharmaceutical company invested millions in autolysis technology to develop faster-acting vaccines.

definition: 1. Occurring singly; not one of a pair, as a vein or muscle.2. odd, unpaired.

sentences.sentence: The azygous vein runs singularly down the right side of the vertebral column without a paired counterpart.
The dancers performed an azygous routine, with each partner mirroring the other's movements in perfect symmetry.
